segments.s262-g281: رهش (compound of ره + -ش) here means ‘his release/letting go’ and functions as the object of بازدادند. بازدادند is a past-tense transitive verb ‘they gave back/let go’ (third-person plural). The coordinating و links this clause to بگریختند, another past action: ‘and they fled.’
segments.s262-g282: به آورد با او نیاویختند is a negative past statement: نیاویختند (third-person plural negative) means ‘they did not cling/throw themselves (upon).’ به آورد is adverbial/directional (‘into the fray’/‘to the encounter’) modifying نیاویختند; با او marks the opponent ‘with him/at him.’ The whole means they did not engage/attack him.
segments.s262-g283: به بالا برآمد is a simple past clause: برآمد ‘he rose/appeared’ with به بالا indicating upward direction (‘up/on high’). به کردار مست is an adverbial simile: به + کردار (manner/conduct) + مست (‘drunk, figuratively intoxicated’) = ‘in the manner/behaviour of a drunk.’
segments.s262-g284: خروشش is the noun ‘his roar’ (خروش + possessive suffix -ش) as subject; همی is a poetic continuous/emphatic particle (‘indeed/continually’). کوه را is the definite object marked by را, and کرد here is causative/past ‘made/caused’; پست is the result predicate ‘low’ — ‘his roar made the mountain low/humbled it.’
segments.s262-g285: همی is the habitual/continuous particle ('just/continually'); نیزه برگاشت is a past transitive action: برگاشت (he lifted/took up) with نیزه as direct object. بر گرد سر is a prepositional phrase ‘around the head’ (بر + گرد + سر = around his head), describing the motion of the spear.
segments.s262-g286: که introduces an explanatory/relative clause (‘for/that’). هومان ویسه is a name phrase (Homan of Viseh); است is the copula ‘is’; پیروزگر is an epithet/noun ‘victor, conqueror.’ Together: ‘for Homan Viseh is the victor.’
segments.s262-g287: خروشیدن is a verbal noun/infinitive ‘the roaring’ used as the subject; نای رویین is a noun phrase where نای ‘reed/pipe’ is qualified by رویین ‘iron-hard’ (an adjective), so ‘the iron reed’ (a poetic image for a metal blast or clashing sound). ز دشت is a prepositional phrase ‘from the plain,’ giving origin.
segments.s262-g288: برآمد is a past verb ‘arose/issued forth.’ چو is a comparative/temporal particle ‘as/when,’ introducing the comparison/simultaneous action. نیزه ز بالا بگشت is an elliptical clause ‘the spear (from above) turned/was turned’; بگشت here is the past of برگشتن ‘turned/returned’ — because the provided sense options do not include that meaning, its specific listed-sense index is not applicable.
segments.s262-g297: کز is the archaic/clipped conjunction 'that' introducing a subordinate clause which supplies the object of بدانند in the next segment. ایشان is the subject 'they'. بد modifies the noun phrase این پیشدستی ('this preemptive act, precedence'), and به خون is a prepositional/result phrase 'into/with blood' (i.e. 'leading to bloodshed'); the full noun phrase is fronted here and the verb is postponed to the next segment, a common classical/poetic inversion.
segments.s262-g298: بدانند is 3rd-person-plural present/subjunctive (classical form) of دانستن used here as 'they consider/deem'. The object of بدانند is the noun phrase in the previous segment (elliptical reference). و هم is simply 'and also'. بر بدی رهنمون is a predicative noun phrase: رهنمون 'guide' governed by the preposition بر 'toward/to' and modified by بدی 'evil' — together meaning 'a guide to/leading toward evil'; thus آنان 'consider (it) ... a guide to evil.'
segments.s262-g299: از آن پس is a temporal phrase 'from then on / after that' (از + demonstrative آن + پس). به گردنکشان is a prepositional phrase 'at/toward the گردنکشان' (here 'rebels'/'neck-slayers'), and بنگرید is the classical 2nd/3rd-person plural present/imperative form of نگریستن meaning 'look / watch / regard'. Word order places the temporal phrase first, the prepositional phrase before the verb, and the verb at the end, a typical classical/poetic sequence.
segments.s262-g300: که تا introduces a subordinate temporal/purpose clause; here تا means 'until'. جنگ او را is the definite/ topicalized phrase 'his battle' (را marks definiteness/emphasis). The second که is a relative/connector 'which/that', آید is 3rd-singular present/subjunctive of آمدن 'comes', and پدید is an intransitive adjective/verb 'appear, come to light' — together 'until his war/battle comes to light.'
segments.s262-g161: از آن پس که is a temporal subordinating phrase meaning 'since that time when'. سوگند خوردی is past tense, 2nd person singular: literally 'you ate an oath' — a classical idiom meaning 'you swore'. به گاه is a small prepositional/temporal phrase 'on the occasion/at the time' that links this clause to the following objects introduced by به in the next segment.
segments.s262-g162: This is a string of prepositional objects introduced by به: 'به خورشید و ماه و به تخت و کلاه' = 'by the sun and moon and by the throne and crown'. The coordinating و links the items; the repeated به before تخت emphasizes the prepositional relationship. کلاه here functions poetically as 'crown'.
segments.s262-g163: که‌گر (classical contraction of که گر) is a conditional 'if/should'. چشم من is the subject 'my eye'. در گه کارزار is a temporal/locative phrase 'in the hour/place of battle' (در = 'in', گه = 'time/occasion', کارزار = 'battle'), specifying when/where the hypothetical sight would occur.
segments.s262-g164: به پیران is the dative/prepositional object 'upon the elders'. برافتد is a 3rd-person subjunctive/optative meaning 'should fall upon' or 'befall' (classical usage). برآرم is 1st-person singular (present/intentional in narrative: 'I will bring/raise'), and دمار is the object 'ruin/calamity' — together 'I will wreak ruin'.
segments.s262-g165: چو here introduces a temporal/simile clause 'when/like'. شیر ژیان is a descriptive simile 'a living (or vigorous) lion' modifying the subject implied with the verb. لشکر آراستی is the verb phrase 'you arrayed/arranged the army' — آراستی is past 2nd-person singular of آراستن/آرايیدن ('to arrange, to set in order').
segments.s262-g166: همی is an emphatic/adverbial particle (here intensifying the desire). بآرزو ('with longing') is an adverbial phrase modifying the verb خواستی. جنگ ما is the object 'battle with us' (literally 'our battle'), and خواستی is past 2nd-person singular 'you wanted/wished' — so: 'you eagerly wanted battle with us.'
segments.s262-g167: کنون is the temporal adverb 'now'. از پس کوه is a locative phrase 'from/behind the mountain' (از = 'from', پس = 'after/behind', کوه = 'mountain'). چون مستمند is a simile 'like a needy/poor one' (چون = 'like', مستمند = 'needy, poor').
segments.s262-g168: نشستی is the main verb addressing the addressee ('you sit' / 'you sat') — here a narrative present/past 2nd-person form (note: the lemma and senses given in the wordlist are for a different verb sense, so this verb's specific 'sit' sense is not listed). به کردار is a prepositional phrase 'in conduct/behavior' (به = 'in/by', کردار = 'conduct'). غرم نژند is a compound adjectival phrase describing that conduct as base/lowly; نژند = 'base/worthless'.
segments.s262-g65: وگر introduces a conditional 'if'. تو نیابی is a present-tense negative (تو + نیا/یاب) meaning 'you are not found' or 'you cannot be found' (second-person). مگر here has the sense 'then/otherwise' or 'in that case' linking the condition to the speaker's reaction. گستهم is a poetically inflected first-person past/present-perfect form 'I wandered/ searched' functioning as the speaker's response in the conditional clause.
segments.s262-g66: بیاید is an optative/subjunctive third-person form meaning 'may come' or 'let it come' (a wish or resultant clause). دمان ('دامان') is a poetic word for the hem/skirt of a garment. با is the preposition 'with'. فروهل here is a proper name (a combatant) mentioned as the one whose hem will meet the speaker's; the lexical entry is archaic but it functions as a name. به هم is a fixed adverbial phrase 'together / to one another' (به + هم = together).
segments.s262-g67: که introduces a relative clause 'who/that'. جوید is third-person singular present 'seeks' or 'desires'. نبردم here is a nominal phrase 'my battle' or 'a fight against me' (the object of جوید, i.e., 'seeks to fight me'); the provided lemma senses do not match this nominal use, so treat it as the noun 'نبرد' with a possessive -م 'my'. ز is the poetic preposition 'from / among'. جنگاوران is the plural 'warriors' (the class from which the seeker comes).
segments.s262-g68: به is the instrumental/agentive preposition here meaning 'by/with' (introducing the weapons used). تیغ 'sword' and سنان 'spear' are listed in an asyndetic series joined by و 'and'. The second به repeats the instrumental relation before گرز گران, where گرز 'mace/club' is qualified by the adjective گران 'heavy' (postpositive adjective used in the classical poetic order).
segments.s262-g69: هر آن کس constructs 'whosoever / every person who...'. که introduces the relative clause. پیش من آید is 'comes before me / appears in my presence' (پیش = before/in the presence of; آید = comes). به کین means 'with hatred / in a spirit of vengeance' (به + noun indicating manner).
segments.s262-g70: زمانه here functions as an agentive/fateful force 'the times / fortune' rather than merely 'era'. بر او is 'upon him/against him' (بر = upon). بر نوردد زمین is a poetic verbal phrase where نوردد (third-person singular) means 'roll over / trample' and زمین 'the earth' is the grammatical subject that is 'rolled over' or used against him — together giving the sense 'time/fortune will cause the earth to roll over him' (i.e., overwhelm him).
segments.s262-g71: وگر introduces another conditional 'if'. تیغ ما را is the object 'our sword' with را marking the definite object (تیغ = sword; ما = our). ببیند is third-person singular present/subjunctive 'he sees' or 'he should see' (here simple 'sees'). به جنگ is an adverbial prepositional phrase 'in battle' (به marking the circumstance).
segments.s262-g72: بدرد is a verb meaning 'to wound / to pierce / to cause pain' in this context. دل شیر 'the heart of the lion' is the direct object phrase (دل = heart, شیر = lion). و links the two coordinated objects. چرم پلنگ 'the hide of the leopard' (چرم = hide/leather, پلنگ = leopard) completes the parallelism: the sword will gore both lion's heart and leopard's hide.
segments.s262-g9: که is the relative pronoun 'who/that' introducing a descriptive clause. دانا is the noun/adjective 'wise' as the clause's subject. به هر کار is a prepositional phrase 'in/with respect to every affair' (به = to/for; هر = every; کار = affair). سازد is a classical 3rd-person present/subjunctive form of ساختن 'makes/does' and here combines with درنگ (a noun) to mean 'causes/has a pause' or more idiomatically 'hesitates/pauses'; word order is subject + adverbial/object + verb.
segments.s262-g10: سر اندر نیارد is literal 'does not put his head into' with نیارد here functioning as a negative verb form (classical/poetic) meaning 'does not put/insert/venture'. به پیکار و ننگ is a prepositional phrase 'into battle and shame' (به = into/toward; پیکار = battle; ننگ = disgrace), so the clause means he does not risk his head in combat and dishonour.
segments.s262-g11: سبکسار is a noun/adjective 'light‑headed, rash (person)' as the subject. تندی is a noun 'haste' used as the object/what is shown. نماید is the classical 3rd‑person present of نمودن 'shows/displays'. نخست is an adverb 'first/initially' placed after the verb.
segments.s262-g12: به فرجام کار is a prepositional noun phrase 'to/at the outcome of the affair' (به = to/at; فرجام = end/outcome; کار = affair). انده آرد is a two‑word verb phrase: انده (placed/put) + آرد (brings) meaning 'brings/sets (something) to (its) end' or 'renders it settled', and درست is an adjective 'right/correct' qualifying the result.
segments.s262-g13: زبانی is 'a tongue' (metonymically 'a speaker'). که introduces the relative clause 'which/that'. اندر سرش مغز نیست literally 'in his head there is no brain' (اندر = in/within; سرش = his head; مغز = brain; نیست = is not), so the whole clause describes someone who speaks but lacks sense.
segments.s262-g14: اگر introduces a conditional 'if'. در بارد here is an idiomatic/poetic phrase meaning 'if he utters/speaks it' (the listed senses for در and بارد do not match this poetic usage, so they are marked unknown). همان nigh/that refers back to 'that (speech)' and نغز is the adjective 'witty/pithy'; نیست is the copula negative 'is not'. The clause says that even if such a tongue speaks, the result is not witty.
segments.s262-g15: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. هومان is the proper name 'Hūmān' as subject. بدین رزم is the prepositional phrase 'in/at this battle' formed of ب(ـ) + دین (classical demonstrative 'this') + رزم (battle); the segment labels separate the pieces but they jointly mean 'in this battle'. تندی نمود is the verb phrase 'showed/displayed haste' (تندی = haste; نمود = showed/appeared).
segments.s262-g16: ندانم is the fused negative+verb 'I do not know' (نـ + دانم). چه is the interrogative 'what'. آرد is the classical 3rd‑person present of آوردن 'brings' and expresses what will be brought. به فرجام سود is the prepositional phrase 'to/at the outcome (فرجام) [what] benefit/profit (سود)' — overall: 'I don't know what benefit it will bring in the end.'
segments.s262-g217: پس is an initial discourse adverb meaning 'then.' اندیشه کرد is a past-tense verbal phrase ('thought'); Persian often puts the verb کرد after the noun اندیشه to form 'he thought.' اندران پهلوان (literally 'among them the champion') is a topicalized noun phrase: اندرَان ('among them') locates the action, followed by پهلوان (the subject) with flexible word order typical of classical Persian.
segments.s262-g218: The initial که introduces a subordinate indirect question/relative clause ('who/that'). پیشش means 'before him / in his presence.' The second که introduces the relative sense 'who may come' with آید ('comes' / present subjunctive/optative used as future/potential). به جنگ is a directional/purpose phrase 'to battle,' and از گوان is an adverbial phrase meaning 'in that manner / thus' (گوان = 'thus/like that'). The double که reflects classical Persian style where a complementizer and a relative marker both appear.
segments.s262-g219: گر is the conditional 'if.' از نامداران literally 'from the renowned ones' marks selection ('one from the famous men'). هژبری appears here as a proper name or epithet (appositive to نامداران). دمان does not match the ordinary noun senses given in the glosses and here functions as part of the name/identifier; read together as a named person in apposition to 'one of the renowned.'
segments.s262-g220: فرستم is 1st-person present/subjunctive used in a conditional ('if I send' / 'were I to send'); classical Persian uses this after گر to form the protasis. به نزدیک means 'close to / near,' and این بدگمان is the object phrase 'this suspicious one' (این = 'this,' بدگمان = 'suspicious' as an epithet following the noun phrase).
segments.s262-g221: شود is an auxiliary here giving a future/result sense ('will be'); combined with the past participle کشته it yields a passive 'will be killed.' هومان is the patient/name of the person, and بر این رزمگاه is a locative phrase 'on this battlefield' (بر = 'on/upon'). The verb + participle order reflects classical Persian passive formation.
segments.s262-g222: ز is the poetic form of از meaning 'from.' ترکان is the source group 'the Turks.' نیاید is a negative present/subjunctive form 'will not come / will not appear,' and کسی in the scope of the negation functions as 'no one' here. کینه‌خواه ('seeker of vengeance' / 'avenger') follows کسی as its modifier. The whole means 'no avenger will come from the Turks.'
segments.s262-g223: دل پهلوانش is a possessed noun phrase 'the heart of his champion' (پهلوانش = 'his champion' with the -ش possessive). بپیچد is a present/subjunctive (here impersonal/future sense) from پیچیدن 'to twist/turn,' so 'the heart twists' or 'is wrung.' به درد is a prepositional phrase 'into pain' or 'with sorrow.' The verb form conveys a resultant emotional state.
segments.s262-g224: از آن پس is a temporal phrase 'from that time on / after that.' به تندی is an adverbial phrase 'with haste / eagerly' (تندی = haste/fierceness). نجوید is the present negative form of جویدن/جست‌و‌جو 'to seek' (the initial ن negates the verb), so 'will not seek.' نبرد is the object 'battle,' yielding 'after that he will not, in haste, seek battle.'
segments.s262-g129: 'به کینِ سیاوش' is a prepositional phrase meaning 'for/with the purpose of avenging Siyavash' (به + کین + اسم in genitive relation). 'پس از کیقباد' is a temporal adjunct placed after it: 'after Kay Qobad.' Note the Persian order: purpose phrase then temporal phrase; no finite verb appears in this segment.
segments.s262-g130: كسی کو is an archaic/poetic structure meaning 'someone who' (کسی + relative particle کو). 'کلاه مهی' is a compound noun 'moon‑like helmet' (کلاه = helmet/hat; مهی = moon‑like, i.e. shining/beautiful). 'برنهاد' is the preterite/perfect of برنهادن 'to place on/raise onto' (here 'put on'). The whole clause therefore says 'someone who put on the moon‑like helmet.'
segments.s262-g131: کمر بست literally 'he bound his waist' (an idiom: 'he girded himself / prepared'). تا introduces purpose 'in order that / so that' and governs the following clause 'گیتی آباد کرد' meaning 'he made the world prosperous' (کرد = made/caused). The verb کرد completes the purpose clause.
segments.s262-g132: This is a nominal list followed by the verb کرد 'made/appointed'. سپهدار is a title 'commander' and is being predicated of the following names: گودرز and کشواد are presented as the persons so appointed. Here کرد functions as 'appointed/made (into)' and applies to the entire preceding cluster.
segments.s262-g133: همیشه is an adverb 'always'. به پیش کیان means 'in the presence of / before the kings' (به پیش = 'before/in front of' + کیان 'the kings/royal house'). کینه‌خواه is a noun 'avenger' or 'one who seeks vengeance' and here names the figure characterized as present before the kings.
segments.s262-g134: This is a nominal apposition/epithet string describing that avenger. 'پدر بر پدر' is an idiomatic repetition meaning 'father after father' (i.e., generation after generation / ancestor upon ancestor). 'نیو' appears as a name or epithet appended to the string here; 'و سالار شاه' adds 'and the king's commander' (سالار = commander, شاه = king). The whole segment lists titles/epithets for the avenger.
segments.s262-g135: و دیگر 'and another' introduces an additional person. که is the relative particle 'who/that'. از گرز او literally 'by/from his mace' — a figurative agent/instrument phrase meaning 'by his club/mace'. بی‌گمان is an adverb 'undoubtedly/without doubt' modifying the verb that follows.
segments.s262-g136: سرآید is a present/future‑oriented verbal form of سرآمدن 'to come / befall' here meaning 'will fall/befall'. به سالارتان means 'upon/against your commanders' (به = upon/toward + سالارتان = your commanders). بر زمان is a temporal adverbial 'at the appointed time / in due time' (بر = on/upon; زمان = time/era).
segments.s262-g241: ندانی is a negative interrogative verb (literally 'you do not know' or 'do you not know'); که introduces a subordinate clause 'that...'; شیر is the noun 'lion' (subject of the subordinate clause); ژیان here follows شیر as an epithet meaning 'life'/'vital' or idiomatically 'brave' (literal 'of life' but idiomatic sense attached to the lion); روز جنگ is a time phrase 'the day of battle' (روز = 'day', followed by the noun جنگ 'battle'). Word order: verb (ندانی) + complementizer (که) + nominal subject phrase (شیر ژیان) + time adjunct (روز جنگ).
segments.s262-g242: نیالاید is an older/poetic negative/perfect-form verb here meaning something like 'does not (bring/put) / does not sink' (contextually 'does not sink/drive into'); از بن is a prepositional phrase 'from the root/base' (از = 'from', بن = 'root, base'); به introduces the target/goal (here 'into' or 'onto'); روباه is 'fox' (the object/target); چنگ is 'claw' (instrument or part used). Morphosyntax: the verb (نیالاید) governs the following prepositional phrases indicating source (از بن) and target (به روباه چنگ).
segments.s262-g243: و is the coordinating conjunction 'and' linking this line to the previous action; دیگر qualifies لشکر as 'other' or 'another' (here 'two other'); دو لشکر is the numeral + noun 'two armies'; چنین ساخته is a participial or perfect construction 'made/formed thus' (چنین = 'so/thus', ساخته = 'made/formed'). Word order follows Persian norm: conjunction + qualifier + numeral + noun + comment/participial phrase.
segments.s262-g244: همه 'all' quantifies the following group; بادپایان (plural of بادپا) is an epithet 'the fleet-footed' or 'swift runners'; سر افراخته is a compound adjective 'head-raised' = 'with heads held high' (سر = 'head', افراخته = 'raised/lofty'). Together they describe the composition and bearing of the two armies.
segments.s262-g245: به کینه is a prepositional phrase 'out of spite' or 'because of a grudge' (به + کینه = 'because of hatred'); دو تن 'two persons' is the cause-agent reference; پیش سازند جنگ is a verbal clause 'they make/prepare the battle beforehand/forward' (پیش = 'before/in front', سازند here is 3rd plural present/subjunctive of ساختن 'to make/prepare', and جنگ = 'battle'). The clause says the war is set up because of a grudge between two people.
segments.s262-g246: همه 'all' quantifies نامداران 'the famed/renowned ones' (here used as a plural noun, 'the renowned warriors/men'); بخایند is a poetic verb meaning 'open/bare' or 'make manifest' (here contextually 'bared' or 'unsheathed'); چنگ 'claw' is the object or image — together 'all the renowned ones bared their claws' i.e., prepared for combat. Note this is figurative language: بخایند is archaic/poetic and paired with چنگ to evoke readiness to fight.
segments.s262-g247: سپه را is the definite/topic-marked object 'the army' (سپه = 'army, host', را marks it as definite/topic); همه پیش باید شدن is a periphrastic obligation: همه 'all/together', پیش 'forward/in front', باید 'must/ought', شدن 'to become/go' — together 'the whole army must go forward' or 'the entire host must advance.' باید is the modal 'must' and شدن functions as the verbal infinitive/verb of motion in this idiom.
segments.s262-g248: به introduces the target/direction 'into/toward'; انبوه is an adjective/noun 'a multitude, dense crowd'; زخمی is an adjective or substantive 'the wounded' (here a collective); بباید is an older/poetic form of باید 'must' (modal); زدن is the infinitive 'to strike/beat/hit' — together 'one must strike into the dense mass of the wounded' (i.e. attack the crowded ranks). The clause uses the modal + infinitive construction to express obligation of action.
segments.s262-g1: نشست here is used in the epic sense 'alighted/dismounted' (an intransitive past action). از بر زین is a prepositional phrase: از (from/out of) + بر (off/on) + زین (saddle) yields 'off the saddle.' سپیده‌دمان is an adverbial/time expression ‘at daybreak,’ placed after the verb for poetic rhythm.
segments.s262-g2: چو here means 'like/as' introducing a simile. شیر ژیان is a descriptive nominal phrase 'lion [ژیان]' (ژیان functions adjectivally/epithetically here), with ژیان strengthening the image; با یکی ترجمان is an accompanying phrase 'with one interpreter' (با = with, یکی = one/a certain, ترجمان = interpreter). Word order is typical poetic Persian: simile first, then the accompaniment.
segments.s262-g3: بیامد is a simple past 'came.' به نزدیک ایران is a locative phrase 'to/near Iran' with به + نزدیک (near) + ایران (the land); سپاه is the subject 'an army/host' placed after the locative for poetic ordering (common in classical verse).
segments.s262-g4: پر از جنگ is an adjectival phrase 'full of war' modifying سپاه (understood from the previous segment). The sequence دل سر (heart, head) lists internal faculties; پر از کین شاه means 'were full of hatred (kīn) toward/of the king' or 'the king's heart and head were full of hatred' depending on attachment — classically it reads as 'the king's heart and head were full of rancour.' Persian drops the copula, so 'پر' functions adjectivally with 'از' introducing the content.
segments.s262-g5: چو here functions as 'when.' پیران is the subject 'the elders.' بدانست is past 'knew/learned it' (با-دانست composite meaning 'came to know'). کو شد بجنگ is a compressed relative/indirect clause 'who had gone to battle' (کو used classically as a relative 'who/which,' شد = became/went, بجنگ = 'to fight' as infinitival notion).
segments.s262-g6: بر او بر جهان گشت is an inverted construction: گشت (became/turned) is the verb, and بر او/بر جهان indicate the experiential domain ('upon him' / 'in the world'); together the phrase conveys 'the world turned upon him' or better idiomatically 'the world grew narrow for him.' ز اندوه تنگ is a causal phrase 'narrow because of sorrow' (ز = because of, اندوه = sorrow, تنگ = narrow/constricted). The lack of a copula is typical; gapped elements are recovered from prior text.
segments.s262-g7: بجوشیدش is a past/presentive form meaning 'boiled (within him/it)' with the enclitic -ش marking 'him/it.' از درد is a causal phrase 'from pain/sorrow.' هومان جگر is a possessive phrase 'Hōmān's jigar' (jigar here used metaphorically as 'heart/seat of feeling'). Word order places the possessor before the possessed in this poetic line.
segments.s262-g8: یکی داستان یاد کرد is a light-verb construction: یکی داستان (one/a certain tale) + یاد 'remembrance' + کرد 'did' = 'he remembered a tale.' یاد کرد here functions as the verbal phrase 'recalled/remembered.' از پدر specifies the source 'from his father.'
segments.s262-g33: This is a nominal-predicate inversion common in epic Persian: 'سر ویسگان' (head/chief of the Visgān) is placed before the copula and the name. 'هومان' is the logical subject/predicate noun linked by the copula 'است'. 'به نام' is a prepositional phrase meaning 'by name' or 'called'.
segments.s262-g34: 'که' here is a relative pronoun ('whose' / 'who'), linking this clause to Hūmān. 'تیغش' = 'his sword' (تیغ + enclitic -ش). 'دل شیر' is a compound meaning 'heart of a lion' (lion-hearted); 'دارد' is the present-tense verb 'has'. 'نیام' (scabbard) completes the phrase by poetic word order: the scabbard belonging to his sword is described as bearing a lion's heart. The sequence is inverted for poetic effect (possessed noun — attribute — possessed object).
segments.s262-g35: چو is a temporal conjunction 'when'. The verb دیدند (past, 3rd pl.) carries the action; ایرانیان is the agent (subject). 'گرز اوی' is the direct object: 'his mace' (گرز + possession اوی). Word order is inverted for emphasis (conjunction + verb before subject).
segments.s262-g36: 'کمر بستن' is a compound verbal idea 'to gird (one's) loins / to gird on a belt' used here as the action; خسروی (kingly one) is the agent. 'برز اوی' is a noun phrase 'his exalted/high (belt)' with برز as an adjective 'exalted/high' modifying the belt (implicit). The order places the verbal phrase before its agent and then the object for poetic rhythm.
segments.s262-g37: 'همه دست' = 'all hands' i.e. all the strength/effort. 'نیزه‌گزاران' refers to the spear-bearers (agent noun); poetic compounds like this sometimes use -گزاران for those who wield or set spears. 'ز' is the poetic preposition 'from/because of' and 'کار' here means 'the action / the fight'. The whole phrase sets up the subject and cause: all the spear-bearers' strength (in the action...).
segments.s262-g38: فروماند is a past verb/adjective meaning 'were worn out / were left behind'. 'از' indicates cause ('by/from'), and 'فر' is the technical epic term 'royal glory/splendour' (here the cause). 'آن نامدار' ('that renowned [man]') identifies whose glory caused the effect. Together: they were exhausted because of that famed man's far/royal glory.
segments.s262-g39: 'همه یکسره بازگشتند' = 'all returned/withdrew straightaway / entirely' where یکسره intensifies 'بازگشتند' (they turned back/withdrew). 'از اوی' is the source phrase 'from him' (اوی = 'him/it'). The verb is past 3rd plural, and word order places the adverbial 'یکسره' directly after 'همه' for emphasis.
segments.s262-g40: سوی = 'toward'; ترجمانش is 'his translator/interpreter' (ترجمان + enclitic ش). 'نهادند' is the past 3rd pl. of نهادن 'to place/set' used here in the idiom 'روی نهادن' = 'to turn one's face toward' (i.e. to face). 'روی' = 'face', so the phrase means 'they set their faces toward his interpreter' i.e. they faced the interpreter.
segments.s262-g257: Word order is slightly inverted in this epic line: یکی داستان زد جهاندار شاه = “the sovereign king told a certain tale.” زد here is the classical collocation داستان زدن (to tell/recite a tale) — زد is used in the sense of performing/uttering a story. جهاندار شاه (literally “world-ruler king”) is the subject placed after the verb for poetic effect.
segments.s262-g258: به یاد آوردن is the idiom “to bring to mind / to recall.” به is the prepositional element of that idiom, یاد is the noun “remembrance,” and آورم is 1st-person singular present (or narrative) “I bring/recall.” اندرین is an archaic compound (اندر + این) meaning “in this,” and کینه‌گاه is a compound noun “seat/place of hatred.”
segments.s262-g259: که here introduces the clause about the throne (a relative/clausal introducer: “that/about which / concerning which”). تخت کیان = “the throne of the Kayanians/royal house.” جست is used in the sense “seek” (classical infinitive/past sense in narrative), and خواهی مجوی is a conditional/jussive construction: خواهی = “if/would you wish,” مجوی = negative (do not seek) in the imperative/jussive sense. In short: “If you will seek the Kayanian throne, do not seek it (timidly).”
segments.s262-g260: چو here is conditional “if/when.” جویی is used as “search/pursuit” (the act of seeking). از آتش مبرتاب روی is a compressed negative-jussive admonition: مبرتاب (negative form) = “do not turn away / do not shrink from / do not recoil” and روی = “face.” So the clause warns: “if you seek it, do not turn your face away from the fire.”
segments.s262-g261: ترا here is the archaic oblique form meaning “for you / you have” (written in the wordlist as separated elements ت + را); the construction ترا آرزو ... نیست = “you have no desire ...” آرزو is the noun “desire/wish.” جنگ و پیکار is a paired nominal: “war and battle/fighting.” نیست is the copula-negative “is not / there is not,” so the whole phrase means “You have no desire for war and fighting.”
segments.s262-g262: وگر = “but if / and if.” گل چنی = “such as a flower / like a flower” (گل = flower, چنی = such/so). راه = “path/way.” بی‌خار is a compound meaning “without thorns / thornless.” نیست closes the clause with negation: “it is not (without thorns).” Put more naturally: “even if the road were like a flower, it would not be thornless.”
segments.s262-g263: نداری here is the negative possession/readily understood as “you do not have” (classical second-person present negative). ز ایران = “from Iran” (poetic order), and یکی شیرمرد = “a single / one brave man” (یکی = one/a single, شیرمرد = bravely valorous man). The phrase says there is no single brave man from Iran.
segments.s262-g264: که is the relative pronoun “who/that.” با من = “with me,” کند is the verb “does/makes” used here in the sense of performing the action (goes/acts); پیش لشکر نبرد is a prepositional/locative phrase “before the host/in front of the army to fight” (پیش = before/in presence of, لشکر = army/host, نبرد = battle). Together: “who will, with me, go forth to fight before the host.”
segments.s262-g89: اگر introduces a conditional clause ('if'). جنگ گردان is a compound-like phrase meaning 'battle‑host/war‑troop' where جنگ qualifies گردان; بجویی is 2nd‑person singular present (classical/poetic) with the prefix ب‑ + root جوی(دن) meaning 'you seek/are seeking'. همی is an archaic continuative/emphatic particle often attached to verbs in Ferdowsi to mark ongoing or emphatic aspect ('still/indeed'). Word order is Topic (object) before verb, typical in poetic Persian.
segments.s262-g90: سوی 'toward' marks direction. پهلوان is the addressee/target 'champion, hero.' چون here functions as a conjunction 'as/when' (introducing the manner or time of the seeking). بپویی is again 2nd‑person present (ب‑ + جوئی) 'you seek/go in search,' and همی repeats the continuative/emphatic particle. The segment directs the seeker toward the hero: 'seek toward the champion as you seek.'
segments.s262-g91: ز is the poetic preposition 'from.' گودرز is the proper name 'Goudarz' (source/agent). دستوری functions as a noun 'an order/command' (adjective/derivative meaning 'commanding' used substantively). جنگ is the object 'for the battle.' خواه is the present/imperative form of خواستن used here to mean 'seek/ask/obtain' (2nd‑person directive). The whole segment is an imperative request: 'from Goudarz seek an order of battle.'
segments.s262-g92: پس از ما is a temporal phrase 'after us' (پس here = 'after'); به جنگ اندر آهنگ is a prepositional phrase where به جنگ 'toward/into the battle' and اندر آهنگ literally 'in/into intention' i.e. 'make ready/prepare' (آهنگ = intention/aim, here 'readiness'). خواه again is an imperative/optative 'make/do' (from خواستن used as directive). Together: 'then, after us, prepare to enter the fight.'
segments.s262-g93: بدو is the prepositional phrase 'to him' (به + او) placed before the verb. گفت here is the past reporting verb 'said' (classical word‑order: to‑him said Houman). هومان is the subject/name. که introduces reported speech/imperative ('that' / 'saying that'). خیره is an adjective/adverb 'staring/astonished' and مگوی is the negative imperative (مگو) 'do not say' or idiomatically 'do not stand/sit in that stunned way.' In sum: 'Houman said to him: "Do not stand there stunned."'
segments.s262-g94: بدین روی is an adverbial phrase 'with this face / in this manner' (بدین = به + این, an archaic fused form; روی = face/manner). با من 'with me' (instrumental/reciprocal). بهانه 'excuse, pretext' is the object, and مجوی is the negative imperative (مجوی = مَجُو 'do not seek') telling him not to look for/offer excuses. The clause is a prohibition: 'Do not, in this way, seek an excuse with me.'
segments.s262-g95: تو 'you' is the addressee. این رزم را uses the object marker را to mark 'this battle' as the object/topic. جای 'place' is the predicate noun, and مردان 'men' (here: heroes/valiant men) is the group specified. گزین is an imperative 'choose/select.' The verb is imperative directed to the addressee: 'You, choose this battle as the place for men.' Note the verb comes at the end as typical in older Persian.
segments.s262-g96: نه is the negation 'not' (contrastive: 'not for ...'). مرد here (used elliptically) echoes 'mardan' and means 'men/valiant ones' (heroic sense). سوارانی 'horsemen/riders' identifies mounted fighters; و 'and' links the coordination; دشت 'plain' names the place and کین 'vengeance/hatred' characterizes it ('plain of vengeance'). The segment denies that the combat is for mere cavalry or for a field of vendetta: 'not horsemen and a plain of vengeance.'
segments.s262-g145: بدین is the prepositional phrase 'with/by this' (ب as the verbal/prepositional prefix + دین as the deictic unit in archaic spelling). تیغ is the noun 'sword' as the thing being referred to. کاندر is an archaic contracted relative phrase (کَه + اندر → 'که اندر') meaning 'in which/wherein', میان means 'middle', and بسته‌ای is a past/perfect form (poetic/archaic 2nd/3rd‑person perfect shape) meaning 'you have bound/sealed' or 'is bound' — together forming a relative clause 'in which (you) have bound'.
segments.s262-g146: گیا is a noun 'plant/herb' (the thing bound into the sword). بر here is the preposition 'on/upon'. که introduces a relative clause 'which/that'; از + جنگ خود is 'from/away from your battle' (از = from; جنگ = battle; خود = reflexive/own). رسته‌ای is an archaic perfect/passive sense 'you have been delivered/escaped' (i.e. 'you have freed/escaped yourself') completing the clause 'upon which you have been freed from your fighting'.
segments.s262-g147: بدین = 'with this' (ب prefix + دین as deictic); گرز is 'mace/club'. جویی here is the present/poetic 2nd/3rd‑person verbal form 'you/one seek(s)' or 'you go in search (of)' (in classical poetry verbs like جویی function where modern Persian would use می‌جویی). همی is a continuous/emphatic adverb 'still/continually'. کارزار is the object 'battle/struggle'.
segments.s262-g148: که introduces a relative clause 'which/that'. بر here has the sense 'against' (useful/operative versus something). ترگ و جوشن = 'rag and cuirass/armor' (ترگ = rag; جوشن = cuirass). نیاید is an archaic negative present/impersonal 'does not come/does not happen' and together نیاید به کار is the idiom 'does not avail / is of no use'.
segments.s262-g149: وز is an archaic connective (here 'and/thereupon/from that') — a poetic particle not identical to modern از; آنجا = 'there'. بد‌ان is the fused phrase 'ب + آن' = 'with that' (i.e. 'in that state/with that astonishment'). خیرگی = 'astonishment/stare'. بازگشت is the past verbal form 'he returned' (third person past).
segments.s262-g150: تو is the 2nd‑person pronoun 'you'. گفتی here functions as 'you said' (poetic usage; the provided lemma list gives 'conversation' so the verbal sense is the poetic past 'you said/you spoke'). مگر is a modal/rhetorical particle 'perhaps / isn't it that'. شیر is 'lion' as the topic; بدساز is a compound/adjective 'bad‑made / ill‑tempered' (here 'ill‑tempered' or 'badly behaving'). گشت is the past copula/verb 'became' — 'became ill‑tempered.'
segments.s262-g151: کمربسته is a participial/adjectival form 'girded' or 'having girt the loins' (describing warriors ready for action). کین = 'vengeance/hatred' functioning as a postpositive genitive/qualifier, and آزادگان = 'the free/noble ones' (subject). The phrase reads 'the free (آزادگان), girded with vengeance'.
segments.s262-g152: به نزدیک is the locative/prepositional phrase 'near/at the approach of' (به = to/at; نزدیک = near). گودرز is the proper name 'Goudarz' (the person near whom they are). کشوادگان is a group/epithet name (poetic/archaic); here it stands in apposition or as an epithet and likely names that band/party — its exact lexical sense is archaic/uncertain in the provided list.
segments.s262-g113: یکی با من اکنون به آوردگاه is a fronted noun phrase: یکی (here 'someone') is indefinite and placed before the accompaniment phrase با من ('with me') and the temporal اکنون ('now'). به آوردگاه is a direction ('to the battlefield') with به governing the place noun آوردگاه.
segments.s262-g114: ببایدت is the fused archaic necessity form meaning 'it is necessary for you' (you must); it takes an infinitive complement, here گشتن ('to go'). به پیش سپاه is a prepositional phrase 'to/in front of the army' with پیش functioning as 'in the presence of / before' and سپاه 'army' as its object.
segments.s262-g115: به خورشید تابان introduces the beneficiary/goal 'toward the shining sun' (به here = 'towards/into'); برآیدت is برآید + enclitic ت (to you) meaning 'will arise/appear to you'; نام is the subject 'name/reputation' that 'rises'.
segments.s262-g116: که is a subordinating conjunction 'for/that/because', introducing the reason clause. پیش من اندر گذاری تو گام literally orders the elements 'before me (پیش من) in (اندر) place you put (گذاری) your step (تو گام)' — classical word order places the verb گذاری before its object; here گذاری is 2nd/3rd‑person present 'you place/you set', so the clause explains why the name will rise.
segments.s262-g117: وگر is the conditional 'if'; تو نیایی is the negative-form verb meaning 'you do not come' (classical negative present/subjunctive sense); به جنگم here intends 'to my battle/fight' (the phrase functions adverbially) and رواست is روا + ست 'it is fitting/allowable' (روا 'permissible' plus the copula).
segments.s262-g118: زواره گرازه places a proper name (زواره) with a following noun گرازه 'young boar' (apposition); نگر is the imperative 'look!' and تا کجاست uses تا introducing the result/clause 'to see how far/where it is' (تا کجاست = 'how far/where it stands').
segments.s262-g119: کسی را is the direct object 'someone' marked by را; ز گردان 'from the troop/ranks' gives origin; به پیش من 'into my presence/before me' gives direction. آر is here an imperative 'bring' (classical imperative of آوردن) — this sense is not listed in the provided lemma senses, so it is flagged accordingly.
segments.s262-g120: که introduces a relative clause 'who/that'; باشد is a subjunctive/optative copula here meaning 'may be / let him be' (i.e. 'who is'); ز ایرانیان 'from the Iranians' indicates membership; نامدار is the predicative adjective 'renowned'.
segments.s262-g265: 'به چاره' is a prepositional phrase meaning 'to/for a remedy' placed before the verb; 'همی' is the older continuous/affirmative particle that, with the present-subjunctive ending, gives a future/habitual sense; 'بازگردانیم' is first-person plural present-subjunctive (or hortative) meaning 'we will restore/bring back'. The whole segment reads literally 'to a remedy we will restore.'
segments.s262-g266: 'نگیرم' is the present-subjunctive form of 'گیر' with the negative prefix ن-, here functioning as 'I shall not be taken / I will not fall (into)' or 'I will not take' depending on context; the object 'فریبت' (faryabat) is the noun 'your/that deception/trick'; 'اگر دانیم' is a conditional clause 'if we know' where 'دانیم' is present-subjunctive/optative 'we know' used in subordinate condition. Together the clause means roughly 'I will not be caught by your trick, if we know.'
segments.s262-g267: 'همه' ('all') quantifies the noun phrase 'نامداران' ('renowned ones'); the adjective 'پرخاشجوی' follows the noun as is normal in Persian poetry and modifies 'نامداران' ('quarrelsome'/'hot‑tempered'). The phrase therefore means 'all the renowned, quarrelsome men.'
segments.s262-g268: 'به گودرز گفتند' is a simple past (third plural) reported‑speech construction 'they said to Goudarz'; the following 'کاینست' is a contracted 'که اینست' introducing the content of what was said ('that this is ...'); 'روی' here is a noun meaning 'face/appearance' or more loosely 'the situation/demeanor' — i.e. 'this is the situation/this is how he stands.'
segments.s262-g269: که introduces the reported content ('that'). از ما 'of/from us' marks the group; یکی را uses را as the definite/object marker to indicate 'one [person] (of us)' as the object. به آوردگاه is the destination 'to the battlefield' (به = to; آوردگاه = battlefield).
segments.s262-g270: فرستی is the second-person verb 'you send' (here used in reported/requested sense — optative/subjunctive flavor in narrative). به نزدیک او is a locative phrase 'to/near him' (به + نزدیک + او). کینه‌خواه is an appositive noun 'avenger' describing the one being sent.
segments.s262-g271: چنین داد پاسخ is a fixed phrasing 'gave this reply/answered thus' (چنین = thus; داد = gave; پاسخ = reply). The following که introduces the content of that reply. امروز 'today' sets the temporal frame, and روی here is used metaphorically as 'face/appearance' meaning 'prospect/opportunity' in the idiom 'روی ندارد'.
segments.s262-g272: ندارد + شدن (infinitive) is a classical Persian construction meaning 'there is no happening/possibility of X' (ندارد = 'does not have', شدن = 'to happen/become'). جنگ را is the definite object 'the battle' (را marking the object). پیش اوی = 'before/in the presence of him' (پیش = before/in front of; اوی is an archaic enclitic form of 'he/him'). The whole phrase says that a battle will not occur in his presence today.
segments.s262-g81: یکی here is an indefinite 'one/a certain' specifying a single tale. The noun داستان 'story' is the direct object, followed by the genitive phrase از کیان 'of the Kians'. یاد کن is an imperative compound (یاد 'remembrance' + کن 'do') meaning 'recall/relate' — i.e., 'tell'.
segments.s262-g82: ز is the poetic preposition 'from/of'. فام خرد is a genitive phrase 'the hue/trace of wisdom' (فام modified by خرد). گردن آزاد کن is an imperative clause 'free the neck' (آزاد adjective + کن imperative); idiomatically it means 'rid yourself of the coloring/constraint of prudence'.
segments.s262-g83: که introduces a relative/conditional clause ('that/for/when'); the archaic combination هر کو functions like 'هر که' = 'whoever'. به جنگ اندر آید = 'enters into battle' (به…اندر expressing motion/entry into). نخست is the adverb 'first', placed at the end for emphasis.
segments.s262-g84: ره 'way' is the thing to be sought; بازگشتن is the infinitive 'to return'. ببایدش is an impersonal/modal form meaning 'it is necessary for him' (بباید + clitic ش = 'he must'), and جست here is the verb 'seek' (classical form of جستن). The word order places the object 'ره بازگشتن' before the modal and verb: 'one must seek a way of return.'
segments.s262-g85: از اینها 'of these/among these' begins the phrase; که تو نام بردی is a relative clause 'that you named/mentioned' (نام بردی = 'you mentioned', بردی as 'took/mentioned'). بجنگ is an adverbial/infinitival phrase 'in battle' or 'for fighting' qualifying the group.
segments.s262-g86: همه 'all/everyone' is the subject; جنگ را uses را to mark the domain ('in war' / 'regarding battle'); تیز 'sharp/keen' modifies the following noun. دار(ند) is the present 'they have/possess', and چنگ ('claw/talon') is the thing they possess in war — idiomatically 'they are fierce in battle.' Word order places the predicate after the object-marked domain for emphasis.
segments.s262-g87: ولیکن 'but/yet' introduces contrast. چو here means 'if/when' and begins the conditional clause. فرمان سالار شاه is a genitive chain 'the command of the lord king' (فرمان modified by سالار 'commander' and شاه 'king').
segments.s262-g88: نباشد is the negative subjunctive/optative 'is not / does not exist' (dependent on the preceding چو 'if'); نسازد is the negative present/subjunctive of ساختن 'to make/prepare' (ن + سازد). کسی is an indefinite subject 'anyone/no one' and رزمگاه 'battlefield' is the object — together: 'no one will/does make a battlefield.' The verb-before-subject order is typical in such negated/subordinate clauses for emphasis.
segments.s262-g41: که introduces a relative/that-clause ('who/that'); رو پیش هومان is a short prepositional phrase meaning 'facing/in the presence of Homan' (رو = toward; پیش = before/in the presence of). به ترکی زبان marks the medium of speech, literally 'in the Turkish language' (به ... زبان = 'in ... tongue').
segments.s262-g42: همه گفته ما is a poetic/inverted report clause: 'they all said (to us)' with ما functioning as the recipient (to us). The following phrase بر او بر بخوان is reported imperative ('Read/recite over him'); بخوان is the imperative/commandive form 'read/recite'. The doubled بر gives the sense 'upon/against him' (prepositional emphasis).
segments.s262-g43: که begins the clause ('that'); ما را ... نیست uses the existential/possessive construction meaning 'we do not have ...' (ما را marks the person lacking something). به جنگ تو آهنگ نیست literally 'there is no intention toward war with you' — آهنگ here means 'intention/aim'.
segments.s262-g44: ز introduces the source/agent 'from' or 'on the part of' (poetic z-). گودرز is the proper name; دستوری جنگ is a noun phrase 'an order (for) war' and نیست negates existence: 'there is no war-order from Goudarz.'
segments.s262-g45: اگر introduces a conditional clause ('if'). جنگ جوید is a simple present/subjunctive third-person 'he seeks war' (جوید = 'seeks'); گشاده است راه is an example of poetic inversion where the predicate 'گشاده است' ('is open') precedes the head noun 'راه' ('the way').
segments.s262-g46: سوی introduces the direction/complement of راه ('toward'); نامور پهلوان سپاه is a noun phrase 'the army of the famed champion' with نامور qualifying پهلوان and سپاه as the head noun ('army'). Together they complete 'راه سوی ... سپاه' = 'the way toward the famed champion's host.'
segments.s262-g47: ز marks source or agent here ('from' or 'on the part of'). سالار گردان و گردنکشان is a coordinated noun phrase 'the commander of the troops and the گردنکشان' (گردان = troop(s); گردنکشان is a plural epithet often 'rebels' or 'proud ones').
segments.s262-g48: به هومان is the indirect object 'to Homan/for Homan'. بدادند is simple past 'they gave' (subject omitted). یک‌یک نشان is the direct object phrase 'a token, one by one' (یک‌یک = 'one by one'; نشان = 'token/mark'), placed after the verb for emphasis.
segments.s262-g17: جهان داورش is a possessive phrase: جهان (the world) + داورش, where داورش is داور + -ش, literally “its judge” = “the judge of the world.” باد is the optative particle (a classical wish/’may it be’) that follows the subject, and فریادرس is the predicate noun “rescuer/succourer.” The whole segment reads as a wish: “May the world’s judge be a rescuer.”
segments.s262-g18: جز اویش = “except him” (جز = except; اویش = او + -یش, a third‑person possessive/pronominal form used here as ‘him’/‘for him’). نبینم is the present/past narrative negative “I do not/ I saw not / I see no” (here narrative: “I see no”). همی is an emphatic/continuous particle (here reinforcing ‘at all/ever’). یار کس = “a friend/ally (of any one)” where کس after یار is generic ‘anyone’ and the whole clause: “I see no companion/ally besides him.”
segments.s262-g19: چو is a temporal conjunction “when.” هومان ویسه are two proper names (Hūmān and Vīseh). بدان رزمگاه is “to/into that battlefield”: بدَان is a poetic/contracted locative meaning “to that” (here represented orthographically as the preposition ب + the demonstrative element), and رزمگاه is “battlefield.” The clause as a whole: “When Hūmān (brought) Vīseh to that battlefield….”
segments.s262-g20: که introduces a relative/subordinate clause (“where/for/for it was that”). گودرز کشواد are two proper names (Goudarz and Kashvād) placed together; this is apposition/listing of leaders. بد با سپاه here functions as a locative/participial idea “had come with an army” — in the poem بد is a poetic/archaic element in this phrase and با سپاه means “with an army.” (The line compresses the narrative; the verbs and particles are poetic and some forms are contracted.)
segments.s262-g21: بیامد is simple past “he came.” که here introduces the clause of intention or narration (“who/that / to ...”), and جوید is the verb “seeks/looks for” (classical present or narrative use: “came seeking”). ز = “from/among” (poetic form), گردان = “troop/contingent,” and نبرد = “battle.” Together: “He came seeking battle among the ranks.”
segments.s262-g22: نگهبان لشکر is a genitive phrase “the guard/protector of the army” (نگهبان + لشکر). بدو = به + او “to him/unto him.” بازخورد in this poetic/narrative context functions as a past/resultative verbal idea “turned back” or “responded/returned” (classical compound verbal sense rather than the modern noun “feedback”). So the segment means “the army‑guard turned back toward him.”
segments.s262-g23: طلایه is the subject “the vanguard/forefront.” بیامد is past “came/arrived.” بر is the preposition “upon/against,” and ترجمان (literally “interpreter/translator” in ordinary usage) here denotes the party/men to whom the vanguard came up — the phrase means “the vanguard came up upon/against the tarjuman.” Word order is subject + verb + prepositional object, typical of narrative poetry.
segments.s262-g24: سواران ایران is a genitive noun phrase “the horsemen of Iran” (possessed noun follows in classical order). همه is a quantifier “all/every one,” and بدگمان is an adjective “suspicious/distrustful.” Together: “All the Iranian horsemen were suspicious.”
segments.s262-g73: چنین (fronted adverb) modifies the verb that follows: “thus/so.” داد is the simple past 3rd-person singular of دادن, here meaning “gave/uttered.” رهام is the proper-name subject; Persian poetic word order often places داد before its subject. پاسخ بدوی is the object phrase: پاسخ “reply/answer” modified by بدوی (here adjectival ‘original/immediate/straightforward’). The whole segment = “Thus Roham gave (uttered) an immediate reply.”
segments.s262-g74: که‌ای is the archaic/poetic vocative/relative form (literally كِه + ای) addressing the addressee: “O who/you who are…”; it introduces what Roham calls the addressee. نامور is an epithet “famed/renowned.” گرد here functions as a verbal/participial element meaning “going about/being given to” (classical usage: گرد + adjective = ‘one who goes about …’), and پرخاشجوی is the adjective “quarrelsome/aggressive.” Together they form a vocative phrase: “O famed one who goes about quarrelling / given to aggression.”
segments.s262-g75: ز ترکان is a genitive/partitive phrase “of/from the Turks.” ترا is the object pronoun “you” (transferred here as تر + را = ترا in classical order); را is the definite/object marker. بخرد is an adjectival/nominal predicate meaning “a wise one” (بخرد = prudent/wise). انگاشتم is the first-person past tense “I supposed/imagined.” The verb انگاشتم governs the whole clause: “I supposed you to be a wise man among the Turks.”
segments.s262-g76: از این‌سان combines از + این‌سان to mean “from/of this sort / in this way.” که introduces a subordinate clause “that …” هستی here denotes being/existence (classical nominal form used for ‘you are’/‘that you are’), and نپنداشتم is the negative past “I did not suppose/think.” The clause thus means “I did not suppose that you were like this.”
segments.s262-g77: که introduces the content clause (“that …”). تنها means “alone/only.” بدین is the classical contracted prepositional phrase (به + این = بدین) meaning “to this”; in the provided tokenization it is split into ب + دین, but syntactically read as the preposition “to/into this.” رزمگاه is “battlefield.” آمدی is 2nd-person singular past “you came.” Overall: “that you alone came to this battlefield.”
segments.s262-g78: دلاور is a vocative/epithet “brave one” or “hero.” به here is the preposition “to/toward”; پیش سپاه is a prepositional phrase meaning “in front of / before the host/army” (پیش = in the presence of / before; سپاه = host/army). آمدی again is “you came.” The phrase says plainly that he came in front of the army.
segments.s262-g79: بر آنی که is an idiomatic/classical construction meaning “you are of such a nature that …” (بر + آنی is a poetic way to introduce a characterizing clause); که introduces the subordinate clause. اندر جهان = “in the world.” تیغ‌دار is an adjectival compound “sword-bearing” (تیغ = sword, دار = having/holding). The clause sets up a general statement about the world: “that in the world sword‑bearing …”
segments.s262-g80: نبندد is the negative 3rd-person present/subjunctive form of بستن used here in a generalizing sense “does not gird / will not bind.” کمر (literally “waist”) denotes the belt/girdle (i.e., the sword-belt). چون = “like/as”; تو = “you”; دیگر سوار = “other rider” (دیگر = other/another, سوار = rider/horseman). The sequence asserts that no other rider binds/girds the belt (i.e., equips himself with a sword) like you do.
segments.s262-g49: که begins this clause as an interrogative/relative particle introducing a question; here it functions like 'where/which'. گردان is the subject 'regiments/troops'. کجایند is the present/plural interrogative verb 'where are they?' (کجا + -یند). و is the coordinating 'and'. مهتر is the second noun 'chief/leader'. کجا + ست (کجاست) is the singular interrogative 'where is he?' — ست is an archaic poetic form of است (copula).
segments.s262-g50: که here again introduces a relative or interrogative clause ('who' / 'which'). دارد is the present-tense verb 'has/holds' (third-person singular). چپ لشکر is a two-word phrase meaning 'the army's left' (left flank); Persian often places adjective/position word before the head noun in older verse. و joins the two objects. دست راست literally 'right hand' and functions idiomatically as the army's right (right flank or principal support). The clause modifies or continues the preceding question about the leader's location/role.
segments.s262-g51: وز آن پس is a temporal sequence 'after that / then' (وز here is an archaic connective and آن پس = 'after that'). هیونی is a descriptive adjective 'beastly/ferocious' modifying تگاور, a noun 'rider/raider/warrior'; the two together form a compound image 'ferocious raiders'. دمان (‘hem/skirt’) is used as a locative/partitive noun 'from the skirts/outskirts' (i.e. from the flank or edge of the field) — the entire group is thus located 'from the skirts'.
segments.s262-g52: طلایه is the noun 'vanguard/forefront'. برافگند is a past tense verb 'threw up/launched' (3rd person singular, perfective). زی is a classical preposition meaning 'from/of/against' (here 'against' or 'toward' the hero in classical usage). پهلوان is the object 'the champion/hero'. Word order is subject omitted (the raiders above) + object + verb, a common poetic arrangement.
segments.s262-g53: که at the start resumes the narrative connection ('so/then' or 'that'). هومان is the proper name (subject). از آن رزمگه is a prepositional phrase 'from that battlefield' (از = from; آن = that; رزمگه = battlefield). چون پلنگ is a simile 'like a leopard' (چون = like/as; پلنگ = leopard) modifying how Homan came forth.
segments.s262-g54: سوی پهلوان آمد is a simple past clause 'came toward the champion' (سوی = toward; پهلوان = champion; آمد = came). ایدر is an adverb 'here', placed after the verb for emphasis. بجنگ is the infinitive 'to fight' (ب + جنگ) used with آمد to express purpose/result: 'came here to fight.' The infinitive after آمد indicates intention.
segments.s262-g55: چو is a temporal conjunction 'when'. هومان is the subject. ز (poetic form of از) + نزد سواران = 'from near the horsemen / from the vicinity of the horsemen' (نزد = near, سواران = horsemen). برفت is a past tense verb 'he departed / went away' (برفت is an older/poetic perfect of رفت/رفت/رفت). The clause sets the time/background for the next action.
segments.s262-g56: بیامد is a past-tense verb 'came' (poetic form, here 3rd singular). به نزدیک is a prepositional phrase 'to near / up close to'. رهام is the proper name 'Roham' (the person approached). تفت in this position appears as a following name or epithet; the lexical senses given (culinary meanings) do not fit here, so it likely functions as a proper name or an epithet attached to رهام. The clause therefore means 'he came up near Roham (Taft),' with the proper name/epithet following.
segments.s262-g57: وز آنجا خروشی برآورد سخت is an archaic/poetic clause meaning 'from there he uttered a loud cry.' وز آنجا functions as a prepositional/locative phrase ('from there'); خروشی is the verbal noun/object 'a cry/roar'; برآورد is a past/preterite 3rd-person singular verb ('brought forth/uttered') used here in the narrative past; سخت is an adverbial intensifier modifying برآورد ('strongly/loudly').
segments.s262-g58: که‌ای پور سالار بیدار بخت is a vocative exclamation introduced by که‌ای (an archaic particle + vocative 'O'), addressing someone as پورِ سالار ('son/descendant of the Salar/commander'). بیدار is an adjective 'awake/alert' applied as a direct appeal, and بخت ('fortune') is used vocatively — 'O fortunate one.' The whole string is direct speech following the cry.
segments.s262-g59: چپ لشکر و چنگ شیران توی lists two metaphoric titles. چپ لشکر is 'the left (flank) of the army' (چپ = 'left' modifying لشکر = 'army/host'). و links the two epithets. چنگ شیران is a metaphor 'the claw/grip of the lions' (چنگ = 'claw' here, شیران = 'lions'), and توی ('among/in') attaches the address to these attributes (i.e., you are among/posses these qualities).
segments.s262-g60: نگهبان سالار ایران توی is a second set of titles in apposition. نگهبان is a nominal predicate 'guardian/protector,' سالار is a title 'commander/chief,' and ایران specifies the domain 'of Iran.' توی again marks the locus/possession ('in/among' or poetically 'thou art'). Grammatically the phrase functions as a nominal predicate describing the addressee.
segments.s262-g61: بجنبان عنان اندر این رزمگاه is an imperative instruction. بجنبان is an imperative/jussive form 'shake!/move!' addressed to the rider; عنان is the direct object 'the reins.' اندر این رزمگاه is a locative prepositional phrase 'in/within this battlefield' specifying where the action should occur.
segments.s262-g62: میان دو صف برکشیده سپاه is a descriptive clause with typical Persian word order where the noun سپاه ('army/host') is qualified by a preposed locative phrase. میان دو صف = 'between two ranks/lines'; برکشیده is a participial/adjectival form 'drawn up/raised' modifying سپاه. Together: 'the army drawn up between two lines.'
segments.s262-g63: به آورد با من ببایدت گشت shows archaic word order and modal necessity. ببایدت is a compound showing obligation 'it is necessary for you' (باید + enclitic -ت for 'you'). گشت here is the main verb 'go/turn' (infinitival or present/future sense after the modal). به آورد با من is a poetic locution meaning 'to come/attend with me' (به + آورد with the following با من), so the line as a whole reads 'you must go/come with me.'
segments.s262-g64: سوی رود خواهی وگر سوی دشت gives two directional options joined by a conditional/adversative particle. سوی رود = 'toward the river.' خواهی here is second-person modal ('if you will/if you wish' or 'you wish/you will'), and وگر = 'but if / or if not' introduces the alternative سوی دشت = 'toward the plain.' The structure is 'go toward the river if you will, otherwise toward the plain.'
segments.s262-g233: مگر here functions as a conditional/negative particle 'unless'. خیره گردند is a third-person plural present (or subjunctive-style) construction: خیره (staring/astounded) plus گردند (become/turn) → 'they become/stand astounded'. جویند جنگ is another present third-person clause 'they seek/ask for battle' (جویند = 'they search/seek', جنگ = 'battle/fight'). The two verbs are conjoined with و 'and'.
segments.s262-g234: سپاه اندر آرند is literally 'they bring (آرند) the army (سپاه) into/within (اندر) …' — classical Persian allows the object (سپاه) before the verb. زان جای تنگ = 'into/onto that narrow place' (زان = 'that/from that', جای = 'place', تنگ = 'narrow'). آرند is third-person plural present 'they bring/lead'.
segments.s262-g235: چنین داد پاسخ به هومان is straightforward: چنین 'thus', داد 'gave' (past), پاسخ 'an answer', به هومان 'to Houmān'. که begins a relative clause 'who/that'. رو here is part of the idiom رو به (رو به + noun) meaning 'turn toward' or 'assume (a posture) toward'.
segments.s262-g236: به گفتار تندی = 'in/with harsh speech' where به is used adverbially ('in/with') and تندی is an abstract noun 'fierceness/harshness'. و در کار نو links 'and' to a second phrase: در کار نو = 'in a new affair/undertaking' (در 'in', کار 'affair/deed', نو 'new'). The relative clause describes Houmān's shift in tone and action.
segments.s262-g237: چو introduces a temporal clause 'when'. در پیش من = 'in my presence / before me' (در + پیش + من). برگشادی is a past tense verb form 'you opened' (برگشاد + -ی ending, addressing the second person), and زبان here means 'tongue' used idiomatically for 'speech' — 'you opened your tongue' = 'you spoke'.
segments.s262-g238: بدانستم is a past tense first-person 'I knew'. از marks the source or basis of the knowledge 'from'. آشکارت is آشکار with the enclitic -ت 'your', here meaning 'your manifest/open expression'. نهان 'hidden' is the thing inferred: the clause literally says 'I knew the hidden from your manifest (speech)' — i.e. I inferred secret things from what you openly said.
segments.s262-g239: که introduces an explanatory clause 'for/that'. کس را ز ترکان نباشد خرد is an existential/negative statement: کس را (literally 'person' with the object/topic marker را) ز ترکان 'from the Turks', نباشد functions as a classical negative existential 'is not / there is not', خرد 'wisdom' — together: 'that there is no one of the Turks (who has) wisdom.' Word order places the predicate noun خرد after the verb, typical of classical style.
segments.s262-g240: کز introduces a relative 'who/that'. اندیشه خویش = 'his own thought' (اندیشه 'thought', خوی + ش = reflexive 'his own'). رامش برد is a compact classical phrase: رامش 'tranquility/peace' (object) and برد (third-person present/past-archaic form) 'takes/gains/derives' — 'whose own thought brings him peace.' The verb برد comes at the end, as is normal in Persian clauses.
segments.s262-g153: بیامد is an archaic past/preterite third‑person singular meaning ‘came’ and heads the clause. یکی after the verb is the indefinite ‘one / a certain person’ (here ‘a man’). بانگ برزد is a light‑verb phrase where بانگ (‘a cry’) is the noun and برزد (archaic verbal form) indicates the action of striking/uttering that sound; بلند modifies بانگ as ‘loud’. Together: ‘a man came and uttered a loud cry.’
segments.s262-g154: که here introduces the reported utterance (like ‘saying:’). ای in combination with a following noun functions as a vocative (‘O …’), so the phrase introduces an address. برمنش is an epithet ‘of noble bearing / noble‑minded’, مهتر is a title ‘chief / headman’, and دیوبند is the place/name after the title; word order places the epithet before the title and the place/name after it.
segments.s262-g155: شنیدم is first‑person singular past ‘I heard’ and serves as the main verb. همه هرچ is a free relative phrase ‘all / everything that…’ with هرچ (‘whatever’) governing گفتی, here a reported past‑tense clause ‘you said’; به شاه marks the indirect object ‘to the king’. The whole segment = ‘I heard everything you said to the king.’
segments.s262-g156: وز is an archaic compressed conjunctional sequence (classical poetic form corresponding to و از ‘and from’) linking to what follows. آن پس = ‘then / after that’. کشیدی is second‑person past ‘you drew / you led’ with سپه را as its definite object (را marks the object) and به راه indicating direction/purpose ‘onto the road’ — syntactically: verb (کشیدی) + object (سپه را) + goal (به راه).
segments.s262-g157: چنین بود is a verbless/predicative construction where چنین (‘thus’) and بود (‘was’, past of بودن) give ‘thus was’. با شاه is the prepositional phrase ‘with the king’. پیمان تو (‘pact / covenant’ + the postposed possessive تو ‘your’) is the noun phrase that functions as the thing being described by the predicate.
segments.s262-g158: به introduces the indirect object or beneficiary ‘to / for’. پیران سالار is a compound/title ‘the elder(s)‑commander’ (پیران ‘elders’ + سالار ‘chief/commander’). فرمان تو (‘your order/command’) is the possessed noun phrase — word order places the possessor تو after فرمان as the normal Persian genitival form.
segments.s262-g159: فرستاده is the noun ‘messenger / envoy’ and is the subject. کامد is an archaic present/past verb form meaning ‘(he) comes / arrived’ (classical usage). به توران marks the destination ‘to Turan’, and سپاه here qualifies the source/agent as ‘of the army’ (the messenger is from the army) — sequence reflects classical flexibility in placing adjuncts.
segments.s262-g160: گزین here is an adjectival/participial notion ‘chosen’ (from ‘to choose’) placed before پور (‘son’), forming ‘your chosen son’ with تو following the noun as the possessive. گیو is the proper name in apposition, and لشکرپناه is an epithet ‘protector/ refuge of the host (army)’ placed after the name as a descriptive title.
segments.s262-g121: چنین داد پاسخ فریبرز باز is a compact, somewhat inverted clause: چنین داد پاسخ = “gave such an answer” (چنین + past verb داد with پاسخ as its object). فریبرز is the agent placed after the verb–object phrase for poetic word order, and باز here is an adverb meaning “again / returned” (i.e. he replied once more). The whole string therefore reports that Fereburz replied in this way again.
segments.s262-g122: که introduces the reported content (’that’ / ‘which’), starting the subordinate clause of the reply. با شیر درنده is a prepositional phrase ‘with / toward a ferocious lion’ (با + شیر + adjective درنده), and کینه مساز is an imperative prohibition: مساز = negative imperative (م‑ + ساز, ‘do not make/harbour’), with کینه as its object (‘hatred, grudge’). Together: ‘that: with a ferocious lion do not harbour hatred.’
segments.s262-g123: چنینست is a copular phrase meaning ‘it is thus / such is’. فرجام روز نبرد is a noun phrase ‘the outcome (فرجام) of the day (روز) of battle (نبرد)’; in poetry the ezâfe linking is omitted and the nouns appear in sequence. The whole segment states a general truth about the result of a day of fighting.
segments.s262-g124: This is a balanced, elliptical statement: یکی شاد و پیروز = ‘one [is] happy and victorious’ (copula ‘است’ omitted, as is common). و دیگر به درد = ‘and the other [is] in pain’ where به + درد = ‘in/to pain / afflicted’. یکی and دیگر set up the contrast between the two contestants/outcomes.
segments.s262-g125: به پیروزی اندر places the circumstance up front: ‘in/at victory’ (به ... اندر both give locative emphasis). بترس is an imperative ‘be afraid / beware’ (present imperative), and از گزند is the object/prepositional complement ‘of harm / injury’. The construction is an admonition: ‘Even when victorious, be wary of harm.’
segments.s262-g126: که introduces the reason/cause here (‘for / because’). یکسان نگردد is a negative present/subjunctive/indicative form (ن‑ + گردد) meaning ‘does not become / is not made equal’. سپهر بلند = ‘the lofty sky/heaven’ (a poetic image for fate). Word order is slightly inverted for rhythm: verb نگردد before the noun phrase. Overall: ‘for the high heaven is not equal (to all).’
segments.s262-g127: درفش 'banner/standard' is the subject. ار is an archaic conditional particle equivalent to modern اگر ('if'). ز من is a prepositional phrase 'from me'. شاه بستد uses بستد (past form) inside the conditional to mean 'takes/seizes' (classical narratives often use past forms in conditional clauses). The clause ends with رواست, a compound of روا ('fitting') + ست (copula 'is'), so the predicate 'it is fitting' comes at the end.
segments.s262-g128: بدان داد is 'gave to that/him' (ب = to; دان here functions as part of the demonstrative/pointing element), followed by داد 'gave' as the verb. پیلان و لشکر is the coordinated direct object 'elephants and army'. که خواست is a relative/subordinate clause 'which he wanted/asked for' (که = that/which; خواست = wanted/requested). Word order: verb after the indirect object phrase and before the relative clause.
segments.s262-g209: This is a copular/relative clause frame: تو (you) is the addressed subject and آنی functions as a predicate nominal meaning 'the one' (poetic/archaic). که introduces the following reported/relative clause; گویی here functions as a present reporting verb ('you say' / 'you claim'). به‌روز نبرد is an adverbial/time phrase 'on the day of battle' (به‌روز = 'on the day' / 'in the day').
segments.s262-g210: This clause gives the reported content. به خنجر uses به instrumentally ('by/with a dagger'); کنم is the first‑person present/subjunctive form of کردن ('I will do / I will make' here declaring the speaker's action); لاله is the direct object ('a tulip' — poetic image); بر کوه زرد is a locative phrase 'upon the Yellow Mountain' with the adjective زرد following the noun (typical Persian order).
segments.s262-g211: Imperative context: یکی 'someone' (indefinite person) is the thing to be sent; با من 'with me' gives accompaniment; اکنون 'now' is a temporal adverb; بدین رزمگاه is the destination 'to this battlefield' — بدین is the fused/poetic form of به + این ('to this'). Word order puts the recipient/destination phrase after the accompaniment and time.
segments.s262-g212: بگرد is an imperative meaning 'search/look for' or 'go seek'; و coordinates the two imperatives/commands. به here marks the target relation and functions instrumentally/toward ('to/at' or 'with' in the sense 'regarding the man with…'); گرز گران is a noun + adjective 'heavy mace'; کینه‌خواه is a postpositive noun/epithet meaning 'the avenger' or 'vengeful one' — the whole phrase therefore: 'seek out the avenger who has the heavy mace.'
segments.s262-g213: فراوان پسر داری is a simple nominal + verb clause in which فراوان qualifies پسر ('you have many sons'); داری is the second‑person present of داشتن 'to have'. ای نامور is a vocative ('O famed one') addressing the addressee; vocatives often appear detached and emphatic in poetry.
segments.s262-g214: همه is the subject 'all (of them)'; بسته is a participial/adjectival use meaning 'girded' or 'tied on' (i.e., they have girded themselves). بر جنگ ما expresses purpose/occasion 'for our battle/against us' and بر کمر is a locative phrase 'at/round the waist' (i.e. they have girded their belts around the waist). The two بر phrases give purpose and location respectively.
segments.s262-g215: یکی را is the definite/marked object ('someone' as the thing sent) using the object marker را; فرستی is second‑person present 'you send'; بر من functions as 'against me' (بر + pronoun = directed/hostile contact); به جنگ 'to battle' completes the idea 'send someone against me to battle.' The verb precedes the oblique phrases in this poetic word order.
segments.s262-g216: اگر introduces a conditional ('if'); جنگ‌جویی is the conditional/agent noun 'if he is a warrior' (literal 'one who fights'). چه is a rhetorical interrogative 'what/why' and جویی here = 'seeking/searching' (either noun or present form), so the phrase questions the purpose of searching; درنگ is 'delay/tarry' and in this elliptical poetic context functions as an admonition 'do not tarry' or a rebuke 'why delay?' — the line is therefore a concise rhetorical challenge.
segments.s262-g177: بدو (to him) is an unstressed prepositional/object pronoun placed before the verb for emphasis; گفت here is the past-tense verb 'said' (the provided sense list did not include the verbal sense). گودرز is the speaker's name. کاندیشه کن is an imperative: a noun (کاندیشه “thought/consideration”) plus the light verb کن (do) giving the command 'think/consider'. Word order is topical (object/indirect object fronted) + verb + imperative remark.
segments.s262-g178: که introduces a content clause ('that/which'); باشد is the classical optative/subjunctive/present-form of بودن used here to mark 'would be/ought to be' (not a plain imperative). سزا functions as a predicate meaning 'befitting/deserved' (literary 'desert'). با تو گفتن سخن is an infinitival construction: با + pronoun + گفتن (verbal noun) + سخن (its object) — literally 'for you to say a speech', i.e. 'for you to say' or 'for you to speak'.
segments.s262-g179: چو is the temporal/conditional 'when/if' and here governs a subjunctive-type clause; پاسخ بیابی uses بیابی (2nd person present/subjunctive of یافتن) meaning 'you find/obtain an answer'. کنون is the adverb 'now' and ز انجمن is a source phrase 'from the assembly' (ز + noun). The verb (بیابی) appears before some adverbial material, a common Classical Persian order.
segments.s262-g180: به بی دانشی is an adverbial phrase 'in ignorance / out of lack of knowledge' (به marking manner). بر نهی here functions as an admonition: نهی is used in the sense of a negative/imperative prohibition ('do not [put/offer]'), with بر (on/upon) indicating the action directed toward the following object; together they mean 'do not put forward' or 'do not utter'. این سخن is the direct object 'this speech/word'. Note the elliptical, proverbial style: the negative sense is carried by نهی rather than an explicit مکُن.
segments.s262-g181: تو بشناس is the 2nd-person imperative 'you, know' (بشناس = know/recognize). کز is the subordinating particle 'that' introducing the content clause explaining what to know. شاه فرمان من is a compact noun phrase 'the command/order of the king' (فرمان followed by the possessor من in postposition, classical Persian word order). The clause as a whole: 'Know that my command is from the king.'
segments.s262-g182: همین بود uses بود (past/auxiliary copula) with همین ('this/just this') to state or emphasize identity: 'this was/is exactly'. سوگند و پیمان من is a coordinated noun phrase 'my oath and my covenant' with the possessive من following the nouns (common in classical Persian). The whole segment asserts that the foregoing is his sworn pledge.
segments.s262-g183: کنون آمدم is first-person perfect/past 'now I have come / I came just now' (آمدم). با سپاهی گران is an instrumental/associative phrase 'with a mighty/heavy army' where سپاهی ('army/host') is modified by the postposed adjective گران ('heavy/important' used poetically as 'mighty'). Classical Persian freely allows such adjective placement.
segments.s262-g184: از ایران is a source phrase 'from Iran'. گزیده is an adjectival/participial modifier 'chosen/selected' and is followed by the quality adjective دلاور ('valiant') and the head noun سران ('leaders, chiefs') — i.e. 'the chosen valiant leaders.' The modifiers precede the head noun, a normal pattern in Ferdowsi's style.
segments.s262-g201: Imperative clause: برگزین is the imperative 'choose/select'. The direct object یکی ('one, someone') precedes the verb (typical Persian word order allows the object before the verb for emphasis). از میان سپاه is a prepositional/source phrase 'from among the army/ranks' specifying where to select.
segments.s262-g202: Relative clause introduced by که 'who/that' qualifying the chosen person. با من is the prepositional phrase 'with me' (من here is the object of با). بگردد is a classical/poetic present/subjunctive form meaning here 'go about / accompany / move with' (i.e. 'accompany me'); به آوردگاه gives the destination 'to/towards the battlefield'.
segments.s262-g203: The clause begins with که used here in the sense 'for/since' introducing a reason or explanatory remark. من is the clause subject 'I'. از + names (فریبرز و رهام) marks the opponents 'against Fariborz and Roham' (classical usage). جنگ is the nominal element 'war/battle' that connects to the following verb بجستم.
segments.s262-g204: بجستم is a poetic past tense 1st-person verb meaning 'I engaged/fought' (an older form used by Ferdowsi). به سان is a fixed simile 'like/as'. دلاور modifies پلنگ, giving the image 'a valiant/heroic leopard' — a standard heroic simile.
segments.s262-g205: بگشتم is a verbal form meaning 'I turned/went/roamed through' (poetic; here 'I will/ I did go through'). سراسر همه انجمن is a fronted adverbial phrase 'throughout the entire assembly/meeting' that specifies the scope of the movement.
segments.s262-g206: Negative existential construction: نیاید 'does not/ will not come' plus کسی with the negation yields 'no one comes'. ز گردان 'from the troop' indicates source; پیش من 'before me' means 'in my presence / to face me' (i.e. none of the troop will step forward before me).
segments.s262-g207: به here functions as 'with/regarding' or 'to' in the sense 'as for Goudarz'. بد بند is a compound idea 'ill-linked / ill-matched' (بد = bad, بند = link/fit), so پیکارشان means 'their fighting/engagement' and the phrase says the fight was a poor match for Goudarz.
segments.s262-g208: A verbless/result clause in which the infinitive/gerund شنیدن 'to hear' and the negated copula ارزید 'was not worth' are used with گفتارشان 'their speech'. Word order is emphatic: 'Their speech was not worth hearing.' (نه before ارزید negates the worth.)
segments.s262-g137: This is a nominal clause with a topicalized direct-object marked by را: سپه را ('the army' with definite-object/topic marker). به ویسه است places the responsibility/assignment 'to Viseh' (به + name) followed by the copula است. فرمان جنگ ('command of war') is the nominal predicate: literally 'the army (را) to Viseh is command (of) war' → 'Viseh is given command of the army in war.' Word order and the copula are typical of classical/poetic Persian.
