segments.s262-g138: بدو = به + او 'to him/unto him' (prepositional/pronominal form). بازگردد is third-person singular present/subjunctive form of بازگشتن used here to mean 'returns/will return.' همه modifies the following nouns and means 'all.' نام و ننگ ('name and shame') means 'fame and disgrace' and are the items that 'return' to him. The clause literal order is 'to him returns all name and shame.'
segments.s262-g139: اگر introduces a conditional clause ('if'). با توام here reads as با تو + ام (poetic spelling تواَم) meaning 'I am with you' (i.e. 'while I am with you' / 'when I am alongside you'). جنگ فرمان دهد places the verb at the end: فرمان دهد is 'issues/orders' (دَهَد = 'gives/ issues') and جنگ is the actor/scene 'war' or 'battle' (poetic personification). Overall: 'If, when I am with you, war issues its command...'.
segments.s262-g140: دلم = دل + ـم (my heart) — the speaker's heart. پر ز = 'full of' (پر + ز, poetic 'full from/of'). دردست here functions as a compound/adverbial meaning 'at hand' or 'immediately available' (دَرِ دست). درمان is the noun 'remedy/cure.' دهد is again third-person singular 'gives' (will give/shall provide). The clause means 'my heart, full from (i.e. with) pain, will give a remedy at hand.' Note: the entry 'م' in the word-list is a bound element (possessive suffix) rather than the listed meanings; its possessive function is the correct reading here.
segments.s262-g141: ببینی is 2nd-person present/subjunctive or jussive form of دیدن with the progressive/present prefix ب-: 'you will see' / 'see'. که introduces the content clause ('that' / 'how'). من is the subject 'I.' سر is literal 'head' (here also idiomatically 'honor'). چگونه ز ننگ = 'how (out) of shame' (چگونه 'how', ز 'from/of', ننگ 'shame'). So: 'See how I (my head) [come out] from shame.'
segments.s262-g142: برآرم is first-person present/subjunctive/optative 'I raise/lift' (بر + آرم). چو is a temporal conjunction 'when.' پای اندر آرم is a poetic way of saying 'when I set my foot in / when I enter' (پای = foot; اندر = into/in; آرم here denotes the speaker's action of placing/entering — the listed senses for آرم in the wordlist point to 'rest' but in context it functions as 'I put/step' in poetic usage). بجنگ is the prepositional/adverbial phrase 'into battle / in the fight' (بـ + جنگ). Altogether: 'I will raise [my head] when I set foot in the fight.'
segments.s262-g143: چنین 'thus' introduces the reported reply. پاسخش = پاسخ + ـش ('his/its answer' or 'the answer of him') — here 'his answer' or 'the reply' — and داد is the past tense 'gave.' هومان is the subject name 'Humān.' که begins the reported speech/quotative clause and بس is an interjection 'enough!' or 'that is enough' marking that Homan cuts off further pleading.
segments.s262-g144: به گفتار = 'by/with speech' (به indicating means: 'by words' / 'from what you say'). بینم is first-person present/subjunctive/optative 'let me see / I shall see.' ترا = تو + ـرا (object 'you' with the object marker را attached) — here 'you' as the object of 'see.' دسترس is predicate/adjectival noun 'within reach, accessible.' The clause is a curt challenge: 'Enough; let me see by your words whether you are within reach.'
segments.s262-g185: This phrase is an address: شما هم (you also). به + noun (کردار) here marks manner — 'in the manner of' — so به کردار روباه پیر = 'in the manner/conduct of the old fox.' وردِ کلام: the prepositional phrase modifies an implied injunction (go/act).
segments.s262-g186: به بیشه در is a motion/location expression: به (towards/into) + بیشه (thicket/grove) + در (in/inside) gives 'into/into the interior of the grove.' از introduces the cause (because of); از بیم + noun = 'for fear of ...' so از بیم نخچیرگیر = 'for fear of the hunter.'
segments.s262-g187: همی is an emphatic/instantial particle (here urging immediacy). چاره سازید is a plural imperative ('make/devise a remedy/plan'); the coordinated nouns دستان و بند are direct objects — 'dastān' (hands/implements) and بند (bonds/cords/fetters) — items to prepare or fashion.
segments.s262-g188: گریزان is a participial/adjectival form 'keeping away/avoiding'; it qualifies the preceding items (dastān و بند): 'hands and bonds (that) avoid/from.' ز is the poetic preposition 'from,' followed by a series of instruments of attack (گرز, سنان, کمند) joined by و = 'from mace and spear and lasso.'
segments.s262-g189: دلیری مکن is a prohibition: دلیری here means 'rashness' or reckless bravery and مکن is the negative imperative 'do not do.' جنگ ما را: جنگ (fight/battle) marked by the object marker را and qualified by ما ('our fight'). مخواه is a negative present imperative/subjunctive 'do not desire/ask for.'
segments.s262-g190: که introduces the reason ('for/because'). روباه (the fox) is subject; با شیر ('with the lion') is a prepositional phrase meaning 'in company with a lion.' ناید is an archaic 3rd-person negative/present form meaning 'does not come/go' (here 'does not go/does not travel'); به راه = 'on the road' or 'along the way.'
segments.s262-g191: چو here is temporal 'when.' هومان is the subject name. ز marks the source of the reply (ز گودرز = 'from Goudarz'); پاسخ شنید is a simple past clause (شنید = 'heard') so 'when Houmān heard the answer from Goudarz.'
segments.s262-g192: چو at the start of this clause is a simile 'like/as.' شیر is the comparison term 'a lion.' اندر آن رزمگه = 'into/within that battlefield' (اندر = in/within, آن = that, رزمگه = battlefield). بردمید is a poetic past tense verb meaning here 'he rushed/charged/seized the field' (an energetic past action).
segments.s262-g97: This is a short prepositional+verb clause meaning 'from there he passed into the army's heart.' وز is an archaic/poetic preposition equivalent to از (here not matched in the provided senses). آنجا is a locative adverb 'there,' به + قلب سپه is the prepositional phrase 'into the heart/core of the army,' and برگذشت is a past intransitive verb 'passed (over/into)' placed at the end as Persian normally does.
segments.s262-g98: This gives the extent/limit reached: دمان (دامان, 'skirt/hem/foothill') is a locative, تا introduces the limit 'as far as/until,' and the sequence ب + دان + روی functions as 'بدان روی' = 'to that side/face' (here segmented irregularly: ب is the prefixed preposition). لشکر is the subject 'the army' and گذشت is the past verb 'passed.' The locative phrase precedes the verb as in normal Persian word order.
segments.s262-g99: به نزد فریبرز is a set phrase 'to the presence of Fariburz / to Fariburz.' با ترجمان means 'with an interpreter' (با marking accompaniment). Word order places the goal/recipient phrase before the accompanying instrument/person.
segments.s262-g100: بیامد is a simple past 'he came.' به کردار is an adverbial phrase 'in the manner/way of' (به used adverbially + کردار 'manner/behavior'), and باد دمان is a simile 'a wind (باد) of the skirts/foothills (دمان)' — i.e. 'like a gust from the skirts.' The whole phrase modifies the verb, describing how he arrived.
segments.s262-g101: یکی is an indefinite 'one / someone,' functioning as the subject 'one (of them).' برخروشید is a past intransitive 'cried out / roared.' کای is the vocative particle 'O' (a poetic exclamation), and بدنشان 'their bodies' is the vocative object being addressed. The verb precedes the vocative phrase in the narrative flow.
segments.s262-g102: فروبرده is a perfect/past verbal form 'has driven down / has plunged / has thrust down.' گردن is the direct object 'neck,' ز is the agentive/ablative preposition 'by/from' in poetic use, and گردنکشان is the agent noun 'neck-cutters' / 'those who break necks' (epithet for violent enemies). Together this yields 'the neck has been thrust down by the neck-cutters.'
segments.s262-g103: This is a coordinated noun phrase listing military equipment and retinue: سواران 'horsemen,' پیلان (پیلان) 'elephants,' and زرینه کفش 'golden footwear' (زرینه modifying کفش). The coordinating و links the items in a standard enumerative order.
segments.s262-g104: This clause expresses possession/existence for the addressee: the split ت + را = ترا ('to you' / 'for you') is the archaic object/possessive particle, بود is the past 'was/were' giving 'you had' or 'there was to you,' با marks accompaniment 'with,' and کاویانی درفش 'Kayanian banner' is the noun phrase in apposition. Together: 'you had ... and the Kayanian banner (was) with you.'
segments.s262-g225: سپاهش = سپاه + enclitic ش (possessive) = “his army.” به کوه is a prepositional phrase “toward/into the mountain(s).” کنابد شود is a two‑verb classical sequence: کنابد (present/poetic verb) with شود serving as an auxiliary/future marker — together meaning “will be made to suffer / will be driven” (poetic causative/attendant sense). Word order places the possessed noun first, the directional phrase next, and the verb complex at the end, typical of Persian poetic syntax.
segments.s262-g226: به جنگ اندرون is a locative/time phrase “in/into the inner fight/battle.” دستِ ما is the noun phrase “our hand/power” with literal ‘hand’ used idiomatically for power/advantage. بد شود is a verbal predicate “will become bad / will fare badly.” The clause thus says that, in the ensuing inner battle, our power/fortune will be unfavorable.
segments.s262-g227: ور is the conditional particle “if.” از introduces a partitive/genitive relation “from/out of.” نامداران این انجمن is a noun phrase “the famed/renowned (men) of this assembly/gathering” (نامداران is plural). The whole segment sets up the conditional subject (‘if from the famed ones of this gathering…’).
segments.s262-g228: یکی کم شود = “if one (person) becomes fewer / is missing” (یکی = one/someone; کم شود = “becomes less / is lacking”). گم شود نام من = inverted word order typical of verse: گم شود (verb “will be lost”) + نامِ من (my name / my fame). Together: if one of those men is lost, my (reputation) will be lost.
segments.s262-g229: شکسته شود is a passive/resultative construction “become broken / be broken.” دل گوان را is the object phrase marked by را: “the heart(s) of the …” (here referring to brave men/companions); به جنگ is the setting “in/through battle.” The verb at the end governs the earlier noun phrase — a common classical arrangement.
segments.s262-g230: نسازند is a negative present/subjunctive (they do not/will not make/reconcile) — here “they will not make [peace/reconciliation].” زان پس means “after that / thereafter.” به جایی درنگ = “to any place they will not tarry/stop” (به جایی = to/at any place; درنگ = pause/delay). The sequence states consequence: once their hearts are broken in battle, they won’t reconcile and won’t remain anywhere.
segments.s262-g231: همان به که is an idiomatic concessive/optative formula meaning “it is better that / rather that.” با او نسازیم uses نسازیم (we do not make/reconcile) with با او “with him,” so “we should not reconcile with him.” کین (placed after the clause) gives the motive/attitude — “out of vengeance / in vengeance.” The clause uses the negative present-subjunctive sense to express counsel.
segments.s262-g232: بر او بر is a doubled prepositional emphasis (both بر = “against/on/toward”) marking the target “upon him.” ببندیم is first‑person‑plural hortative/subjunctive “let us close / let us bind (shut).” راه کمین = “the path/way of ambush.” Together: an exhortation to set up/close the ambush against him.
segments.s262-g25: بپرسید is a past narrative form here ('they asked') rather than an imperative; کین is used attributively with a sense of hostility or vindictiveness ('vengeful/hostile') modifying مرد; پرخاشجوی is an adjective 'quarrelsome/aggressive' describing that man. The whole segment introduces the questioner and the man being asked about.
segments.s262-g26: به خیره is an adverbial phrase: به + خیره gives 'with a fixed/staring gaze' (به used adverbially). سپس به دشت اندر آورده روی is a small clause where به + دشت = 'towards/into the plain', اندر = archaic 'in/into', آورده (perfect participle 'has brought') combines with روی ('face') to mean 'has turned/brought his face' — lit. 'has brought his face into the plain.'
segments.s262-g27: کجا is the interrogative 'where'; رفت is the lexical verb 'go'; خواهد is the future auxiliary 'will' (classical placement after the lexical verb is common in poetry); همی marks continuity or emphasis ('ever/indeed'); چون introduces a simile 'like'; نوند is a proper name or rare term (here functioning as the thing he is compared to). The clause asks rhetorically where he will go and whether he will go like 'نوند'.
segments.s262-g28: This is a list of possessions/instruments introduced by به (here adverbial/associative 'with' or 'by'): به چنگ = 'with a harp (in hand)'; اندرون گرز = 'a mace within' (اندرون = 'inside/within'); و links the phrases; بر زین کمند = 'on the saddle a lasso' (بر = 'on/upon', زین = 'saddle', کمند = 'lasso/snare'). The nouns are presented in compact, poetic word order.
segments.s262-g29: به ایرانیان is the indirect object 'to the Iranians'; گفت here functions as the past verb 'said' (the lemma senses list is lexical but in context it is 'he said'); پس is temporal 'then/afterwards'; ترجمان is the speaker 'the interpreter' (apposition: 'the interpreter said to the Iranians').
segments.s262-g30: که introduces reported speech ('that'); آمد گه is a fixed idiom meaning 'the time/occasion has come' (آمد = 'has come', گه = 'time/occasion'); گرز و تیر و کمان is a simple enumeration 'mace and arrow and bow' — the clause reports that the moment for these weapons has arrived.
segments.s262-g31: که introduces a content clause 'that'; این is the demonstrative 'this'; شیردل is a compound adjective 'lion‑hearted' (brave); نامبردار is an epithet 'named/renowned' (famed); مرد is 'man' with heroic nuance here (used as 'hero/valiant man'). The segment identifies the subject of the reported statement.
segments.s262-g32: همی is an emphatic/continuative particle ('indeed/ever'); با شما = 'with you' (i.e., against you); کرد + نبرد is a light-verb construction where کرد 'to do/make' combines with the noun نبرد 'battle' to mean 'to wage battle' — in prose one would expect نبرد خواهد کرد ('will wage battle'), but here poetic inversion places کرد and the future auxiliary خواهد before the verbal noun for meter: کرد خواهد نبرد = 'will make/wage battle.'
segments.s262-g193: This clause begins with the indirect object preposition + name (به گودرز گفت = 'he said to Goudarz'), followed by a conditional introduced by ار (if). نیایی is the negative present/imperative-stem form of نیا ‘not come’ and بجنگ is the infinitive/imperative 'to fight' — together ار نیایی بجنگ = 'if you do not come to fight.' Word order is subject omitted after the quoted speech framing.
segments.s262-g194: تو با من introduces the addressee relation 'you with me'. نه زانست is the archaic/poetic negation + copular phrase (نه زان است = 'it is not from that/it is not so'), functioning to deny the following judgement. کایدت is کاید + -ت (your plotting/your craft), and ننگ is the predicate 'shame' — together the clause denies that the addressee's plotting is shameful because he is with the speaker.
segments.s262-g195: از آن پس که is a temporal clause meaning 'since the time that'. جنگ پشن دیده‌ای combines جنگ 'battle' with پشن 'back' (an older/dialectal word) and دیده‌ای (perfect of دیدن) 'you have seen' — literally 'you have seen the back of battle' (i.e. you saw the battle retreat or turned away).
segments.s262-g196: سر از رزم ترکان بپیچیده‌ای is a verbal phrase with سر (head) + از رزم ترکان (away/from the Turks' fight) and the perfect/past participial-like verb بپیچیده‌ای ('you have turned/twisted away'). The idiom سر از ... بپیچیدن means 'to turn away from ...', so the whole says the addressee has turned away from fighting the Turks.
segments.s262-g197: به لاون به جنگ is a locative/time specification 'at Lāvon, in battle'. آزمودی is the past form of آزماییدن 'tested/tried', and the following separated morphemes (م / را) mark the object (here scanned as clitics in the source); together آزمودی مرا means 'you tested me.' The verb is in the past (perfective), recounting a past trial in battle.
segments.s262-g198: به آوردگه is another locative phrase 'at the muster/place of assembly' (or 'in the field'), بر ستودی is the past of ستاییدن 'you praised' with بر here a classical prepositional particle often used with verbs, and again م/را mark the object 'me' (مرا). The two halves contrast a testing in battle with subsequent public praise.
segments.s262-g199: This conditional opener uses ار 'if' followed by the particle ایدونک and هست 'is' to assert the present state ('if it is so/if it stands so'). اینک 'now' focuses the time, and گویی همی is a modal/quotational phrase meaning 'you seem/you might say' (گویی here signals appearance or reported speech; همی gives iterative/continuous flavor).
segments.s262-g200: وز is an archaic poetic variant of از 'from/because of' here (classically used in poetry), followed by این کینه 'this grudge'. کردار جویی is a compound where کردار 'deed/action' is modified by جویی (from جستن/جوییدن) 'seeking/searching' — together 'seeking action' or 'seeking to avenge by deeds'. همی again gives a continuous or emphatic aspect.
segments.s262-g273: چو introduces a temporal subordinate clause 'when'. The clause places the verb برگشت (past tense 'returned') at the end as normal Persian word order: subject هومان + prepositional phrase ز گودرز ('from Goudarz') + verb برگشت. The adverb چیر ('where/there') follows the verb to indicate location/point of occurrence.
segments.s262-g274: برآشفت is a past/inchoative verb meaning 'became angry/was roused'. The phrase بر سان is a poetic simile 'بر + سان' = 'like/as', followed by the noun phrase شیر دلیر ('brave lion') which is the standard post-nominal adjective order (noun then adjective). The whole segment is the main clause predicating Hūmān's reaction.
segments.s262-g275: بخندید is a simple past verb 'he laughed'; it is coordinated with the following clause by و. روی ('face') appears before the prepositional phrase از سپهبد ('from the spahbed'), and the verb بتافت (literally 'shone') is used poetically here to indicate turning/turning one's face (an idiomatic poetic use). The subject remains the same throughout the coordinated clauses.
segments.s262-g276: سوی introduces the directional prepositional phrase 'toward'; روزبانان لشکر is a genitive-like sequence 'the heralds of the army' (possessor follows), and شتافت is a past tense verb 'he hastened/ran'. The verb continues the same subject from the previous clause, so the sentence strings actions together using simple past verbs.
segments.s262-g277: کمان را به زه کرد is a causative/perfective construction 'he made/put a string to the bow' = 'he strung the bow'; را is the definite object marker attached to کمان. The coordination و links this clause to the next; ز ایشان چهار is an elliptical phrase 'from them four', where ایشان ('them') is the target/group and the numeral چهار ('four') is placed after the preposition phrase, leaving the verb omitted until the next segment.
segments.s262-g278: بیفگند is a past verb 'he cast/threw' (here understood as 'shot/threw arrows'); ز اسب 'from (on) the horse' indicates the action was done while mounted, and اندر آن مرغزار 'into that meadow' gives the destination. The object (arrows/four) is understood from the previous segment, so this clause completes the action.
segments.s262-g279: چو marks a temporal subordinate clause 'when'. آن روزبانان لشکر is the subject 'those heralds of the army' with the determiner آن; ز دور is an adverbial 'from afar' placed before the verb, and بدیدند is the past plural verb 'they saw'. This is standard SOV structure with adverbials preceding the verb.
segments.s262-g280: زخم is the direct object 'wound'; سرافراز is an epithet 'triumphant/lofty' placed after the noun as a post-nominal modifier; تور here is the proper name Tūr, positioned after the adjectival phrase in a poetic genitive/appositive construction (the ezafe is omitted in verse). Together they denote 'Tūr's triumphant wound' as the thing seen.
segments.s262-g105: به ترکان (prepositional phrase) comes first and marks the beneficiary/recipient 'to the Turks'. سپردی is second-person singular past ('you handed/entrusted'); it takes the following noun phrase به‌روز نبرد ('the day of battle') as its object. به‌روز here is a poetic modifier meaning 'of the day' and combines with نبرد to mean 'battle-day' or 'day of battle'.
segments.s262-g106: ایلانت (subject) denotes 'your/these heroes' (epic plural). به ایران is a locative/advantage phrase ('in/for Iran' — here 'in Iran'), and نخوانند is a third-person plural present (negative form) of خواندن used with the meaning 'to call/name'. مرد is the predicate noun 'men' (i.e. 'called men'). The clause literally: 'They do not call (them) men in Iran.'
segments.s262-g107: چو is the conditional/time marker 'when/if'. سالار is 'commander/chief'. The two verbs باشی شوی are a poetic doubling of the copula/verb 'to be/become' in the second person singular ('you are / you become'), and زیردست functions as a predicate complement meaning 'having subordinates' or 'being over underlings'.
segments.s262-g108: کمر بندگی is a compound 'belt/girdle of servitude' (کمر = girdle, بندگی = servitude). را is the definite-object marker attached to that noun phrase. ببایدت is the fused modal 'it is necessary for you' (بباید + clitic -ت = 'you must'), and بست is the past stem of بستن 'to bind' used after the modal, so together they mean 'you must bind/tie (it)'. Object precedes the modal+verb here (typical in classical Persian).
segments.s262-g109: سیاووش is the topical/subject name. رد را uses رد ('rejection/exile/passing') with the object marker را; here it specifies the condition/quality in which Siyâvash is linked to the addressee. برادر توی literally 'brother among you' or 'your brother' is the predicative phrase: 'Siyâvash is (your) brother in that condition.' This is poetic, so the condition phrase ('رد') is foregrounded before the predicate.
segments.s262-g110: به گوهر is a prepositional phrase meaning 'in essence' or 'by nature' (به + گوهر). ز سالار is the comparative marker 'than the commander' where ز = 'from/than' in poetic usage. برتر is the comparative adjective 'superior'. توی at the end points back to the addressee ('you'), so the clause reads 'you are superior in essence to the commander.' Word order is inverted for poetic effect.
segments.s262-g111: تو is the (omitted) subject 'you'. باشی is the copular verb 'you are' (second-person form). سزاوار introduces the predicate 'deserving/worthy', and کین خواستن is a verbal-noun construction 'to seek/ask for vengeance' (خواستن functioning as a nominalized verb meaning 'seeking/asking'). Together: 'You are worthy of seeking vengeance.'
segments.s262-g112: به کینه is a prepositional phrase 'with/into hatred' (marking the instrument or manner). ترا is split into clitic ت (second-person object) + را (definite/object marker) and means 'you' as the thing to be acted upon. باید is the impersonal/modal 'it is necessary / you must', and آراستن is the infinitive 'to array/adorn/equip' used here in the sense 'to arm/prepare'. Linear order puts the instrument phrase before the modal and infinitive: 'You must array (yourself) with hatred.'
segments.s262-g249: تو (explicit vocative) + اکنون (time adverb) precede the directional phrase سوی لشکرت; the clause ends with the imperative باز شو ('become/turn back'). لشکرت is لشکر + enclitic -ت marking 'your army'. Word order is topical (تو اکنون) then direction (سوی لشکرت) then verb (باز شو imperative).
segments.s262-g250: برافراز is an imperative meaning 'raise' or 'hold up'; گردن is the direct object 'neck'; به marks the beneficiary/dative 'to'; سالار نو = 'new commander' where نو is an attributive adjective following the noun. The order is verb + object + prepositional phrase indicating recipient.
segments.s262-g251: کز here functions as 'from / out of' (poetic register) introducing the source 'ایرانیان'; چند qualifies جستم as 'many / several' and جستم is first-person past of جستن meaning 'I sought' or 'I made for' — with نبرد as its object 'battle(s)'. The phrase literally: 'From the Iranians I sought several battles.'
segments.s262-g252: This is a verbless/eidetic clause meaning 'there was no one before me except the cold wind.' نزد پیش من functions as a locative/expressing 'in my presence' or 'before me'; کس 'person/anyone' is negated implicitly by جز ('except') plus the following nominal از باد سرد 'from the cold wind.' Persian poetry often omits an explicit copula (was/is) here.
segments.s262-g253: بدان here is a fused/deictic locative ('in/at that') introducing رزمگه 'battlefield'; بر شود (بر + شود) is a periphrastic passive/impersonal 'will be raised/come upon' and نام تو is the patient/theme 'your name.' Together: 'In that battlefield your name will be raised.' Note that the element 'ب' is the prefix in the fused locative and thus not a separate lexical verb.
segments.s262-g254: ز is the poetic preposition 'from'; پیران 'the elders' is the source; برآید (present/future sense) means 'will come forth / will arise / will be granted'; همه کام تو = 'all your desires' (همه modifies کام). The clause says that all the addressee's desires will come from the elders.
segments.s262-g255: بدو 'to him' (dative) + گفت (here the verb 'said') with the subject هومان (proper name). The phrase به بانگ بلند is an adverbial 'with a loud cry/voice' (به + بانگ + بلند). Word order is indirect object + verb + subject + manner phrase — a common poetic ordering.
segments.s262-g256: که introduces the quoted content (equivalent to 'saying/that'); بی 'without' governs کردن 'doing' and کار 'the deed/work' (بی کردن کار = 'without doing the work'); گفتار 'speech' is the topic and چند is a rhetorical 'how much' (i.e. 'how much speech?'). The whole rhetorical question asks about the value of talk without action.
segments.s262-g169: به کردار = a fixed adverbial phrase meaning 'in the manner of / like' (here 'به' functions adverbially with کردار). نخچیر (preceding کردار) is the noun 'prey, hunted game' that the relative clause will describe. کز is the classical fused relative 'که ز' = 'which/from which', and شرزه modifies شیر (the next word) meaning 'rampaging/insolent' (so 'the fierce/ferocious lion'). The clause is left open and completed by the following participle 'گریزان'.
segments.s262-g170: گریزان is a present-participial adjective ('fleeing') that attaches to نخچیر introduced just before. و simply connects the contrasted predicate about the lion. شیر is the subject of the following predicate, and the prepositional cluster از پس اندر (= 'from/behind, within') places the lion spatially: 'the lion (is) brave from behind/while pursuing.' دلیر is a predicate adjective ('brave') describing شیر.
segments.s262-g171: گزیند is the classical third-person present (narrative) of گزینیدن/گزیدن 'chooses' (here rendered 'he chooses' or 'he takes'). به بیشه درون is a prepositional phrase 'into/onto the grove, inside' with درون ('inside') reinforcing location. جای تنگ is the noun + adjective 'a narrow place' (note adjective after noun, standard Persian word order).
segments.s262-g172: نجوید is negated present (ن + جوید) meaning 'does not seek' or 'does not pursue'. ز تیمار جان is a causal/motivating prepositional phrase 'out of care/concern for his life' (ز = 'from/because of', تیمار جان = 'care for life'). نام و ننگ are the coordinated objects 'renown (fame) and shame' — the line says he does not seek fame (or avoid shame) for the sake of life. The phrase thus expresses motive: because he cares for his life he does not pursue fame/reputation.
segments.s262-g173: یکی is an indefinite 'one' meaning 'one (unit/contingent)'. لشکرت is 'your army' (لشکر + 2nd-person suffix ت), and را is the definite/direct-object marker attached for emphasis. به هامون is the locative prepositional phrase 'on/at the plain'. گذار here functions as an imperative/hortative 'place/leave' (classical poetic imperative of گذاشتن), so the whole is a command: 'Put/leave one of your forces on the plain.'
segments.s262-g174: چه داری is a rhetorical question 'what do you have / what need have you' (چه = interrogative 'what', داری = 'you have'). سپاه is the object 'an army/force'. از پس کوهسار is a spatial adjunct 'behind the mountain/ridge' (از پس = 'from/behind', کوهسار = 'mountain, highland'). The line asks why he keeps troops behind the mountain.
segments.s262-g175: چنین بود is a simple past copular phrase 'such/so was'. پیمانت is 'your pact' (پیمان + 2nd-person suffix ت). با شهریار is the prepositional complement 'with the king'. Together: 'Such was your covenant with the sovereign.' Note the possessive suffix -ت attached to پیمان marks 'your'.
segments.s262-g176: که introduces the content/result clause 'that ...'. بر کینه گه is a compact phrase meaning 'on/for the occasion of hatred' or 'out of hatred' (کینه = grudge/hatred, گه = place/occasion). کوه گیری is a verbal compound 'taking/seizing the mountain' (کوه + گیری, verbal noun/infinitival sense), and حصار ('fortress') states the purpose/result: 'to seize the mountain as a fortress' or 'to make a stronghold of the mountain.' The line describes the pact's content: to seize the mountain in enmity.
segments.s262-g289: This is a prepositional/genitive phrase headed by the poetic preposition ز (z). The structure is 'ز + noun (شادی) + genitive chain (دلیران توران سپاه)'; دلیران توران modifies سپاه (the head), so the whole phrase means 'because of / from the joy of the brave men of Turan's host.'
segments.s262-g290: همی is a classical adverbial particle indicating continuous or emphatic action. ترگ is a concrete noun ('rag/shred') acting as the object/image. سودند is an archaic 3rd‑person plural verb form; here it governs the action described and takes the following locative phrase بر چرخ ماه as its place adjunct ('upon the sky/moon'). The overall sense is pictorial and idiomatic (poetic register).
segments.s262-g291: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'; هومان is the subject proper name; بیامد is the past tense 'came.' The following components ب + دان form the demonstrative/locative sense ('to/at that'), so 'چو هومان بیامد بدان چیرگی' = 'when Homan came to that domination.'
segments.s262-g292: بپیچید is a past verb ('twisted/turned') with the prefixed ب used here as a simple past form; گودرز is the subject that follows the verb (common in poetic word order). زان is a deictic 'from that/thereof,' referring back to the previous clause, and خیرگی is the noun 'astonishment' that names what affects Goudarz.
segments.s262-g293: This is a nominal + adjectival predicate: سپهبد is the subject ('the spahbed/general'); پر از + noun is an adjectival phrase ('full of shame'); گشته is a perfect/auxiliary meaning 'became' or 'has become,' and دژم is a predicate adjective 'sullen/grim.' The clause reads 'the spahbed, filled with shame, became sullen.'
segments.s262-g294: گرفته is a past participial/adjectival form meaning 'seized/possessed (by)'; بر او is a prepositional phrase 'upon him'; خشم و تندی ستم is a compound noun phrase 'anger and the fierceness/harshness of injustice' serving as the thing that has seized him. Word order places the predicative participle first, then the affected person and the afflicting emotions.
segments.s262-g295: به ننگ is an oblique phrase 'for/to the shame' or 'from shame,' از دلیران 'from/of the brave' locates the shame among the warriors. بپالود is a past verb 'he purified/cleansed' and خوی is its object 'disposition/temper.' The clause literally says he 'purified his nature from the shame among the brave.'
segments.s262-g296: The second clause repeats سپهبد as subject. یکی اختر is 'one star' (a single notable foe in the poet's metaphor), افگند is the past verb 'threw/cast/struck down,' and پی here is an adverbial 'after/in succession.' Together they express 'the spahbed cast down one star after another' (i.e. slew opponents in succession).
